In order to account for the possibility of some low-level contribution from the host galaxy to the observed radio flux densities of the outflow, we add a host component to the spectral fitting that is described by F_ , host = F_ 0 1.4 GHz ^ _0 , where F_0 is the flux density measured at 1.4 GHz ( F_0<0.41 mJy) and _0 is the spectral index of the host galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper links 9495-3702 to rotating ionized gas, optical variability from TDE AT2019azh, and an EELR whose ionization predates that recent disruption event.

quotes.quote: The ionized gas kinematics show a range of properties with some (8938-6102, 9495-3702) showing evidence of rotation and others (10845-12701, 11965-3703) displaying asymmetric biconical patterns. These may be evidence of outflows, as indeed 10845-12701 is known to host an AGN outflow McElroy2015 . In one case (11965-3703), the ionized gas velocity red/blue components are aligned with the orientation of the EELR; in another (10845-12701), the ionized gas velocities are oriented 45 degrees from the EELR. Future study of the kinematics of the ionized gas as well as other gas phases will yield useful constraints on the impact of past AGN activity on each galaxy.

The sixth galaxy, 9495-3702, returned high _ QSO values of 12.94 and 29.82 in the and g bands respectively, but it returned even higher _ NOT \ QSO values of 72.70 and 33.97. The origin of this variability is the Tidal Disruption Event (TDE) AT2019azh (ASASSN-19dj, Hinkle2021 ).

9495-3702 is the host galaxy of the Tidal Disruption Event (TDE) AT2019azh/ASASSN-19dj Hinkle2021 . The observed optical, UV, and X-ray flare in 2019 was driven by the accretion of debris from an individual star after the star was tidally disrupted by the SMBH. This event was too recent to have ionized the EELR.

sources.summary: The paper identifies KUG 0810+227 as the nearby post-starburst host of AT 2019azh and uses host-galaxy spectroscopy and scaling relations to estimate the central SMBH mass.

quotes.quote: The location of the event ( Gaia J2000 coordinates = 08^ hr 13^ m 16.96^ s , = +22^ 38' 53.99'' ) is consistent with the center of the nearby galaxy KUG 0810+227 which has a redshift of z = 0.0222240 0.0000071 Almeida2023 , corresponding to a luminosity distance of 96.6 Mpc. This galaxy was preselected by as a possible TDE host given its post-starburst properties Arcavi2014 . The event was later classified as a TDE by , based on its brightness, high blackbody temperature of 30,000 K, a position consistent with the center of the galaxy (with an angular offset between the ZTF coordinates of the event and the host nucleus of 0.07 0.31 ), multiple spectra showing a strong blue continuum, and lack of spectroscopic features associated with a supernova (SN) or AGN.

We retrieved the spectrum of the host galaxy from SDSS Data Release 18 Almeida2023 . Estimates of the SMBH Mass derived the SMBH mass of the host of AT 2019azh using the M - relation from with the velocity dispersion measured from the WHT spectrum presented here. We do not consider this definitive evidence favoring one model or the other since the host-galaxy-derived SMBH mass strongly depends on the choice of scaling relation and the spectral resolution used to infer the velocity dispersion, as is evident in the comparison to other works.

gmw_00001746.name: M59
gmw_00001746.overview: M59 is a luminous Virgo elliptical galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0604065, arXiv:2405.04651] that is radio-silent [arXiv:2405.04651] and forms a tight pair with NGC 4660 [arXiv:0803.3181]. It features a disky structure [arXiv:astro-ph/0409061], a counter-rotating core [arXiv:1105.4809, arXiv:astro-ph/0508631], and a rich system of approximately 2,000 globular clusters [arXiv:astro-ph/0604065].

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 4621 as a nearby elliptical with disky structure, blue nuclear features, a known kinematically decoupled core, and possible merger or bar-driven origins.

quotes.quote: NGC 4621 The closest galaxy of the four is NGC 4621 (7 Mpc). It is also the only galaxy classified as an elliptical and is the only galaxy from the sample detected with IRAS (in the 12 m band). In the investigated range the galaxy is disky. The b_ 4 coefficient steadily rises from the center to the distance of 140 pc when it drops, but never reaching negative values. With increasing radius it rises again, implying an outer disk.

The color images reveal the most interesting features in the nucleus. The few central pixels are clearly much bluer than the rest of the bulge (Table  t:color ). Another striking characteristic of the galaxy, mentioned in section 2.3.2, is the extended blue and red features visible on B-I (Fig.  f:nuc_disk ) and V-I images.

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC 4621 to illustrate model constraints, finding a mass-to-light ratio about twice a Milky Way IMF, one of the steepest IMFs, and high [Na/Fe].

quotes.quote: In Figure fig:mcmc we show the covariance between several derived parameters and the mass-to-light ratio, M/L , for NGC 4621. The latter quantity is shown in units of the mass-to-light ratio assuming a MW IMF, (M/L)_ MW .

It is also evident from this figure that the derived M/L is much larger than expected for a MW IMF. Indeed, NGC 4621 has one of the steepest IMFs in our sample (a Salpeter IMF is 60 heavier than a MW IMF, and so NGC 4621, with an M/L that is twice as heavy as a MW IMF, has an IMF even steeper than Salpeter). NGC 4621 also has one of the highest [ Na/Fe ] values in our sample.

It is important to notice that even when allowing the [ Na/Fe ] abundance to reach very high values, a bottom-heavy IMF is still favored for this particular galaxy. In other words, the very strong NaI feature is due both to high [ Na/Fe ] abundance and a preponderance of low-mass stars.

sources.summary: The paper models M59-UCD3 as the most massive known ultracompact dwarf, estimates a several-million-solar-mass black hole, and argues its profile suggests a stripped-galaxy remnant.

quotes.quote: We examine the internal properties of the most massive ultracompact dwarf galaxy (UCD), M59-UCD3, by combining adaptive optics assisted near-IR integral field spectroscopy from Gemini/NIFS, and Hubble Space Telescope (HST) imaging. We use the multi-band HST imaging to create a mass model that suggests and accounts for the presence of multiple stellar populations and structural components. We combine these mass models with kinematics measurements from Gemini/NIFS to find a best-fit stellar mass-to-light ratio ( M/L ) and black hole (BH) mass using Jeans Anisotropic Models (JAM), axisymmetric Schwarzschild models, and triaxial Schwarzschild models. The best fit parameters in the JAM and axisymmetric Schwarzschild models have black holes between 2.5 and 5.9 million solar masses.

We also present deep radio imaging of M59-UCD3 and two other UCDs in Virgo with dynamical BH mass measurements, and compare these to X-ray measurements to check for consistency with the fundamental plane of BH accretion. We detect faint radio emission in M59cO, but find only upper limits for M60-UCD1 and M59-UCD3 despite X-ray detections in both these sources. The BH mass and nuclear light profile of M59-UCD3 suggests it is the tidally stripped remnant of a 10 ^ 9-10 M _ galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 4621 as a massive Virgo early-type galaxy whose spectral residuals reveal Mg/Fe enhancement and whose abundance-ratio proxy corresponds to roughly +0.25.

quotes.quote: Our subsample of galaxies (NGC 4387, NGC 4473, NGC 4489 and NGC 4621) cover a range in age, metallicity, abundance ratio and mass, as these galaxies were selected alongside the Colour-Magnitue Relation of the Virgo cluster. For the most massive galaxies, NGC 4473 and NGC 4621 we see in Fig. fig:galaxies positive residuals for the Fe features, e.g., Fe4383, Fe5270, Fe5335, and negative residuals for the Mg feature at 5170 . This shows the well known [ Mg/Fe ] enhancement, which is characteristic of massive galaxies (e.g., Peletier 1989; Worthey, Faber Gonz alez 1992; Gonz alez 1993).

As an example, Table 5 of Yamada et al. lists [ Z _ Mg /Z _ Fe ] 0.6 for NGC 4473 and NGC 4621. Following Fig. fig:heuristic this translates to [ Mg/Fe ] +0.25 on the abundance ratio scale of Thomas et al. (2003).

sources.summary: The paper presents M59-UCD3 as a very dense compact galaxy, more luminous than M60-UCD1, with high surface density, young age, and a likely stripped-galaxy-center origin.

quotes.quote: The first object discovered, M59-UCD3, has a similar size to M60-UCD1 (half-light radius of r_ h 20 pc) but is 40 more luminous ( M_V 14.6 ), making it the new densest-known galaxy.

For M59-UCD3, the surface brightness (averaged within ) is 2.7 10^4 L_ , pc ^ 2 . This is twice as high as the previous galactic record-holder, M60-UCD1, making M59-UCD3 a prime target for SMBH follow-up. M59-UCD3 is a younger system (see below), reducing its stellar mass-to-light ratio, with a stellar surface mass density of 7 10^4 M_ pc ^ 2 that is 50 higher than that of M60-UCD1.
We thus conclude that M85-HCC1 is most likely a tidally stripped galaxy-center, and also argue the same for M59-UCD3 based on its properties that are closely analogous to M60-UCD1.

sources.summary: The paper selects M59-UCD3 as one of two dense UCDs for a tentative emission estimate, applies an MSP correlation using structural parameters, and predicts fluxes too low for Fermi-LAT detection.

quotes.quote: Taking advantage of these similarities, a tentative estimate of the ray emission for a couple of UCDs was performed. To have an idea of their ray luminosities, some of the densest known UCDs were chosen: M59-UCD3 liu2015most and M85-HCC1 sandoval2015hiding and the N_ MSP correlation discussed in section results was applied. Using their central luminosity densities and core radii (the core radius r_ core was assumed here as 1/5 r_ half , the half-light radius, which is very close to the mean value for the ratio r_ core /r_ half in GLOBCLUST catalog) given by , their values for and then L_ were estimated (Table Table3_UCD ).

To estimate their energy fluxes, isotropic emission was assumed and the distances to M59 and M85 were taken from . Despite their high luminosity, these compact galaxies are so far away that their energy fluxes are extremely low, not detectable by the Fermi-LAT.

sources.summary: The paper lists M59-UCD3 among observed UCDs confirmed to contain black holes and finds simulated stripped-nucleus black hole masses consistent with observed UCD black hole measurements.

quotes.quote: Figure fig:bhmasses plots derived black hole masses against derived total masses for simulated stripped nuclei with black hole masses above 10 6 M _ for the seven massive clusters in EAGLE and UCDs whose black hole content has been studied. The 5 UCDs that are confirmed to contain black holes are M60-UCD1 Seth2014 , VUCD3, M59cO Ahn2017 , M59-UCD3 Ahn2018 and UCD3 Afanasiev_2018 . Also included are UCD 330 and UCD 320 Voggel_2018 , for which only upper limits on black hole masses have been found. Figure fig:bhmasses shows that the black hole masses of the simulated stripped nuclei are consistent with the black hole masses of observed UCDs. The masses and mass fractions of these black holes in UCDs are a reliable indicator of them being stripped nuclei.

sources.summary: M59 is emphasized as a radio-silent Virgo elliptical where collective bounds already rule out substantial transmitter parameter space and individualist searches are weak.

quotes.quote: In fact, collective bounds already rule out a substantial chunk of M59's EIRP-abundance parameter space being probed by the GBT. This follows from how faint it is in the radio, less than 40 \ ^ 1 . GBT's advantages in M59 are for a ``corner'' of rare, sub-Kardashev II transmitters ( 10^ 24 10^ 27 \ ), and ultrarare bright transmitters, where the need to detect at least one broadcast limits the collective bound because of the relatively narrow bandwidth of VLSS ( 42 \ ; Condon98 ). Even the advantage for ultrarare lines could be eliminated by measuring M59's diffuse emission across a few gigahertz of bandwidth. Of course, the issue is not just that the collective bounds are strong for the radio-silent M59, but that the individualist bounds are weak because of the great distances and insensitivity to faint broadcasts.

sources.summary: NGC 4621 is an elliptical with a barely resolved counter-rotating core, younger metal-rich central pixel, extended disc, constant outer age, and steep metallicity decline.

quotes.quote: NGC 4621 = VCC 1903 This elliptical galaxy is known to host a counter-rotating core ben1990,wer2002 , however its size is only 60 pc or 1 arcsec wer2002 and it is barely visible in our data. However, we do observe an younger age and higher metallicity in the central pixel, in agreement with the central, blue colour observed by kra2004 using HST WFPC2 data. It has an extended disc, seen from the isophote analyses e.g. ben1990 . Except for the central pixels, the age is constant along the radius, however its metallicity decreases steeply.

sources.summary: The paper argues that M59-UCD3 likely belongs to the stripped-CSS category because it closely matches M60-UCD1 in mass, size, dispersion, and super-solar stellar populations.

quotes.quote: We also note that the newly discovered object M59-UCD3 Sandoval15 probably fits in this category, since its measured properties make it a virtual clone of M60-UCD1; its stellar mass (2.4 vs 2.3 10 ^8 M _ ), size (20 vs 27 pc), and most importantly its extremely high velocity dispersion ( 70 km s ^ 1 from our MODS spectrum vs 68 5 km s ^ 1 ; Strader13 ) mean that this object is very likely to host a supermassive black hole like M60-UCD1. Our two spectra (from MODS and ESI) result in consistent stellar population parameters, which are also consistent within the uncertainties with the analyses of Sandoval15 and Liu15b . The latter study reported a slightly higher velocity dispersion of 78 km s ^ 1 . Similar to M60-UCD1, M59-UCD3 also has super solar metallicity and [ /Fe ] .

sources.summary: The paper includes M59-UCD3 as a Virgo UCD with evidence for a central massive black hole, a Kroupa-like IMF result, and differing fit parameters from V17.

quotes.quote: Among the UCD sample we include VUCD3 and M59-UCD3, which have evidence for central massive black holes see Table tab:literature_vals for black hole masses Ahn_2017,Ahn_2018 .
UCDs are more bottom-heavy (VUCD3, VUCD7) or Kroupa-like (M59-UCD3, VUCD4).

The most significant difference is for M59-UCD3, where we find _ IMF =1.1 0.2 , compared with _ IMF =1.7 0.4 in V17 (however the variable-solution (M/L) _* is very similar). Although we also find evidence that the IMF is bottom-heavy ( _1>1.3 in Figure fig:paper_metallicity_trends ), our best-fit solution has a larger age and lower metallicity than V17, which leads to a larger (M/L) _ V, MW and hence lower _ IMF .

sources.summary: The paper uses M59 as a bright Virgo elliptical example, noting its rich globular-cluster system, central surface brightness, and large half-light radius for resolved-population requirements.

quotes.quote: M59 is another luminous elliptical which is considerably less luminous than M87, but still one of the brightest elliptical galaxies in Virgo. M59 contains around 2000 globular clusters, easily 10 times more than the Milky Way. It has a central surface brightness, I _0 =15.8 mag arcsec ^ 2 (fairly typical for the giant elliptical galaxies in Virgo). M59 is one of the largest elliptical galaxies with a half-light radius, R _e = 39^ '' (3.25kpc).

sources.summary: The paper classifies NGC 4621 as a power-law galaxy, notes central saturation in the ACS image, and says the VCS pure model falls below central data.

quotes.quote: VCS Models of Power-law Galaxies NGC 4434, 4464, 4621, and 4660 are galaxies that we have classified as power-laws that lf fitted with pure laws. The VCS models for all four galaxies fall below the data as 0 (Figure fig:acscomp2 ). Again, this behavior is echoed in VCS Figure 103, which shows that the fits fall below the observed domain ACS VCS data for <0 3 in NGC 4464, 4621, and 4660, and <0 15 in NGC 4434. lf note that the ACS F475W images of both NGC 4621 and 4660 are actually centrally saturated.

sources.summary: The paper finds M59 lies roughly 2 Mpc in front of M60 and NGC 4638, with distances consistent with NGC 4660 forming a tight pair.

quotes.quote: Taken at face value, the SBF distances suggest that M59 and NGC 4660 lie roughly 2 Mpc to the foreground of M60, NGC 4638, and the bulk of the Virgo cluster. The statistical analysis confirms that the CG is, indeed, the result of a chance alignment of its galaxies, with NGC 4638 lying at least 800 kpc further away (with 99 confidence) than either M59 or NGC 4660. The first two galaxy distances are consistent with M59 and NGC 4660 constituting a tight pair. The dominant galaxy, M60, is at least 440 kpc more distant (95 confidence) than the M59+NGC 4660 pair, and over 1 Mpc (99 confidence) more distant if one uses the broken linear calibration of the SBF distances.

sources.summary: The paper presents NGC 4621 as an illustrative early-type galaxy whose central velocity kink resolves into a counter-rotating core, adding it to the set of KDC-bearing systems.

quotes.quote: Figure fig:sau_oas_comp b shows the stellar velocity field of NGC 4621. The `kink' in the zero-velocity contour observed with is resolved into a clearly counter-rotating core, which was previously found using with AO wernli02 .

To the 9 KDCs we have already considered in the above discussion, we add four objects: NGC 4621 and NGC 5982 - these objects appear to have KDCs from our observations (see section sec:oas_sau_comp ); and NGC 7332 and NGC 7457 - although not observed with , these galaxies show small (unresolved) but distinct central twists in their velocity fields Paper III, see also falcon04 .

gmw_00001747.name: NGC 1379
gmw_00001747.overview: NGC 1379 is a low-luminosity elliptical galaxy located in the Fornax cluster [arXiv:astro-ph/0101080, arXiv:astro-ph/0511770]. The galaxy features a small inner disk [arXiv:astro-ph/9806331], a low Sersic index [arXiv:astro-ph/0702595], and a centrally depleted globular cluster system [arXiv:astro-ph/0101080].

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 1379 as a Fornax elliptical whose globular cluster system is centrally depleted, estimating that about 74 percent of its original clusters were lost.

quotes.quote: NGC 1379 NGC 1379 is an EO galaxy in the Fornax cluster studied by, e.g., Harris Hanes (1987), Kissler-Patig et al. (1997) and later F98b. Harris Hanes (1987) compared the radial profile of the GCS in NGC 1379 with the surface brightness of the galaxy itself as given by Schombert (1986). Their study was limited to the radial range 5-35 kpc, and they did not detect any difference between the two profiles. This was confirmed by Kissler-Patig et al. (1997) that used ground-based data over the 3-10 kpc range. The recent analysis of F98b, based on data of the HST WFPC2, confirms the similarity between the two surface profiles in the outer regions but, at the same time, show as the two differ in the inner regions.

The observed decreasing behaviour toward the galactic centre of the GCS distribution of NGC 1379 seems to indicate that the dynamical depleting mechanisms are efficient, in this galaxy, up to significantly large radii.
Hence NGC 1379 could have lost about 74 of its initial population of globular clusters.

sources.summary: FCC 161 is described as a bright elliptical ancient infaller near NGC 1399, with small rotation, high central dispersion, and line-strength gradients linked to dispersion.

quotes.quote: FCC 161 (NGC 1379) -- Fig. fig:FCC161map FCC 161 is one the brightest elliptical galaxies in the high-density region of the cluster. It is located at R_ proj =0.5^ from FCC 213 (NGC 1399) on the western side of the cluster. It is one of the ancient infallers in the north-south clump of galaxies. Similarly to FCC 147, which is the other giant elliptical galaxy of this cluster region, also the stellar component of FCC 161 is characterised by a small maximum rotation ( v_ max 30 km s ^ 1 ) and a high velocity dispersion in the centre ( 150 km s ^ 1 ).

As for FCC 147, the line-strength maps of Mg b and Fe5015 are characterised by an increasing gradient toward the centre, which is consistent with the gradient measured in the stellar velocity dispersion. The H index in the central parts is 1 \ smaller compared to the outskirts.

sources.summary: The paper studies NGC 1379 as a low-luminosity Fornax elliptical whose globular cluster system was imaged with MOSAIC and treated as embedded in NGC 1399's cluster system.

quotes.quote: Received ; in original form firstpage - lastpage 2005 We present a photometric study of the globular cluster systems of the Fornax cluster galaxies NGC 1374, NGC 1379, and NGC 1387. The data consists of images from the wide-field MOSAIC Imager of the CTIO 4-m telescope, obtained with Washington C and Kron--Cousins R filters. The images cover a field of 36 36 arcmin, corresponding to 200 200 kpc at the Fornax distance. Two of the galaxies, NGC 1374 and NGC 1379, are low-luminosity ellipticals while NGC 1387 is a low-luminosity lenticular. Their cluster systems are still embedded in the cluster system of NGC 1399.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 1379 as a low-luminosity Fornax early-type galaxy whose globular-cluster population, blue-cluster fraction, and specific frequency are measured with NGC 1374 and NGC 1387.

quotes.quote: We present the combined results of two investigations: a large-scale study of the globular cluster system (GCS) around NGC 1399, the central galaxy of the Fornax cluster, and a study of the GCSs around NGC 1374, NGC 1379 and NGC 1387, three low-luminosity early-type galaxies located close to the centre of the same cluster. As there were several low-luminosity early-type galaxies with their own GCSs in the western field (NGC 1374, NGC 1379 and NGC 1387), we decided to keep this field to study them , and we used the eastern fields, where there were no conspicuous galaxies, to study the NGC 1399 GCS over a larger field . In fact, the fraction of blue clusters, with respect to the total GC population is low for the three systems, but even lower for NGC 1387 (43 , 45 , and 24 for NGC 1374, NGC 1379 and NGC 1387, respectively). By means of the luminosity functions we estimate the total GC populations in NGC 1374, NGC 1379 and NGC 1387 between 200 and 400 clusters, and obtain specific frequencies S_ N = 2.4, 1.4 and 1.8, respectively.

sources.summary: NGC 1379 has broadly symmetric kinematics with small-scale asymmetries, anti-correlated RC/VDP extrema, evidence for a small inner disk, and a possible central distinct component.

quotes.quote: NGC 1379 The kinematics of this galaxy, although rather symmetric in its overall appearance, presents a number of remarkable small-scale asymmetries. The series of minima and maxima in both the RC and the VDP are clearly anti-correlated, the maxima of the VDP corresponding to minima in the RC and vice-versa. There is evidence for a small (5 ) inner disk, coming from the steepest part of the RC, clearly connected to the inflexion in both sides of the VDP at this radius. There is also marginal evidence for a central distinct component corresponding to the inflexion of the RC.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 1379 as a low-Sersic-index elliptical whose B-band light profile is fitted to test density-potential-force models for stellar systems.

quotes.quote: In the present study, we focus on the bulge in the center of the Milky Way and the two galaxies from the sample used in Terzi Graham (2005): an elliptical galaxy NGC 1379, and an elliptical galaxy with partially depleted core -- NGC 3348. The Milky Way's bulge and the first galaxy, NGC 1379, with S ersic index of n=2.0 (see Table 2 of Terzi Graham 2005), were chosen because it illustrates that Dehnen is inadequate to describe galaxies with low S ersic indices, and that Prugniel--Simien model is a good alternative.

figd , the 3 parameter Dehnen and Prugniel--Simien models were fitted to the deprojected major-axis K band light-profile of the Milky Way's bulge (first row) and B band light-profile of NGC 1379 (second row).

gmw_00001748.name: J0731+4459
gmw_00001748.overview: J0731+4459 is a high-redshift quasar host galaxy situated at a redshift of z=5.01 [arXiv:2310.18395]. Observations with JWST show that the host galaxy is undetected in the F410M band, marginally detected in the F430M band, and characterized by low galaxy-scale interstellar extinction [arXiv:2310.18395].

sources.summary: The paper constrains the J0731+4459 host galaxy with JWST, finding no F410M detection, marginal F430M flux, galaxy-mass limits, and low inferred interstellar extinction.

quotes.quote: The host galaxy of J0731+4459 ( z=5.01 , top row of Figure fig:psf-subtracted ) is undetected in F410M (measured flux at the <3 level) and marginally detected ( 4 ) in F430M. We report the upper limit on the J0731+4459 F410M flux in Table tab:measurements , but do not use this band to constrain the galaxy mass.

J0731+4459 also has an F410M flux, but because its F410M PSF-subtracted image displays more severe PSF subtraction artifacts than its F430M image, suggesting some degree of mismatch between its quasar and stellar PSF in that band, we use F430M to calculate its mass instead. Assuming that the two quasars in our sample without large far-infrared excesses lie in relatively typical z 6 galaxies, these low extinction levels support our assumption that the galaxy-scale interstellar extinction is low in J0731+4459 and J1120+0641.

gmw_00001749.name: J1340+2813
gmw_00001749.overview: J1340+2813 is a high-redshift galaxy at z=5.36 that is undetected in the F210M band and marginally detected in the F410M and F430M bands [arXiv:2310.18395]. The galaxy exhibits a very large far-infrared luminosity indicative of intense nuclear star formation and obscuration in its central regions [arXiv:2310.18395].

sources.summary: The paper reports marginal JWST detections and mass limits for the J1340+2813 host, with possible strong star formation and dust affecting its inferred stellar mass.

quotes.quote: J1340+2813 ( z=5.36 , second row) is undetected in F210M and marginally detected (4-5 ) in F410M and F430M. For J1340+2813, we use the band at the most similar wavelengths, F410M.

Effect of Intense Nuclear Star Formation Three members of our sample, J1148+5251, J1340+2813, and J2239+0207, have very large far-infrared luminosities indicative of star formation rates >> 1000 M _ yr ^ 1 Leipski2014, Izumi2019, Bertoldi2003 . J1340+2813, J2239+0207, and J1148+5251, however, have very high far infrared luminosities Leipski2014, Izumi2019, Bertoldi2003 and should have significant obscuration in their central regions.

gmw_00001750.name: HSC J2239+0207
gmw_00001750.overview: HSC J2239+0207 is a sub-Eddington quasar located at a redshift of z=6.25 [arXiv:2310.18395]. Its host galaxy is detectable in PSF-subtracted NIRCam images and exhibits high far-infrared luminosity [arXiv:2310.18395].

sources.summary: The paper describes HSC J2239+0207 as the clearest host-galaxy detection, with F360M/F480M excess emission, visible PSF-subtracted host flux, and high far-infrared star formation.

quotes.quote: Our first quasar analyzed, HSC J2239+0207 ( z=6.25 ), is a sub-Eddington quasar with a high ALMA dynamical mass Izumi2019 , making its host galaxy theoretically easy to detect in PSF-subtracted NIRCam images. The exception is J2239+0207, where the quasar exhibits slight excess emission compared to the star in the F360M and F480M bands: as discussed in S23, this is evidence of a host galaxy detection.

J2239+0207 ( z=6.25 , third row) is undetected in F210M but returns fluxes at the 6.8 and 5.5 level in F360M and F480M respectively (as discussed in S23), our only apparently unambiguous detection with this PSF normalization method. That is, the only images in Figure fig:psf-subtracted where the host galaxy emission can be unambiguously seen are the F360M and F480M filters of J2239+0207.
J1340+2813, J2239+0207, and J1148+5251, however, have very high far infrared luminosities Leipski2014, Izumi2019, Bertoldi2003 and should have significant obscuration in their central regions.

gmw_00001751.name: ULAS J112001.48+064124.3
gmw_00001751.overview: ULAS J112001.48+064124.3 is a radio-quiet quasar at a redshift of approximately 7.08 [arXiv:1311.1686, arXiv:2401.10328]. The host galaxy exhibits irregular features and an offset quasar position, suggesting a recent merger [arXiv:2309.04614].

sources.summary: The paper searches X-ray data for lobes around ULAS J1120+0641 at z=7.1, finding no significant quasar-centered linear structure and treating a nearby weak feature as a practical upper limit.

quotes.quote: ULAS J1120+0641 Neither the Chandra image (which is short, 16 ks) nor the XMM-Newton pn + MOS image data ( Page et al 2014; total exposure of 300 ks which on reanalysis gives 120 ks of useable, low background, exposure) shows any significant linear structure around the quasar (Fig. 1). A linear structure which is 1 arcmin long (projected length of 318 kpc at z=7.1 , corresponding to a true length of >385 if inclined at <60 deg to our line of sight) is seen about one arcmin to the WSW, centred on a weak point source. The structure either side of the weak source is at a statistically significant flux above the background (4.3 sigma), but we note smaller blobs (possibly sources) at the same surface brightness level and suspect that it may be due to source confusion.

It is difficult to deduce a precise significance for any weak extended structure but note that in this and other XMM images we see no similar linear features. Where lines do appear they correlate with chip gaps. The structure does not align with a chip gap or anything on the exposure map. The linear feature is present below about 1.5 keV and is best seen between 0.3-1 keV. We shall take this structure as representative of the upper limit of what is undetectable.

sources.summary: The paper re-evaluates the z=7.0842 quasar J1120+0641, arguing its Lyman-alpha emission may fall within matched lower-redshift quasar distributions and may not require damping-wing absorption.

quotes.quote: quasars: individual: J1120+0641 \ \ quasars: emission lines \ \ intergalactic medium \ \ cosmology: observations \ \ dark ages, reionization, first stars The reionization of hydrogen in the intergalactic medium (IGM) is believed to coincide with the buildup of the first galaxies. Determining when and how the IGM became ionized can therefore deliver unique insights into the earliest epochs of galaxy formation. Evidence from the forest in high-redshift quasars indicates that the process largely finished by z 6 Fan06, Becker . While the exact timing remains unknown the recent Planck results Planck favour models of late reionisation, which motivates the observational search for signs of reionisation. The recent discovery of the most distant quasar known, Mortlock , with a redshift of 7.0842 0.0004 Venemans , provides a unique opportunity to probe the epoch of reionisation at a time when the process was potentially just ending. As noted by Mortlock , this object exhibits strong absorption on the blue side of its emission line. The red side of its line may also be somewhat weaker than expected based on the strength of its other emission lines, notably . These traits have jointly been interpreted as evidence for neutral gas in the vicinity of , with the weak line being due to damping wing absorption extending to _ rest 1216 . In a scenario where the neutral gas is intergalactic, models of the quasar's proximity zone suggest that the surrounding IGM may be 10 per cent neutral Bolton .

Among lower-redshift quasars chosen to more closely match in velocity and equivalent width, we find that falls within the observed distribution of emission line strengths. This suggests that damping wing absorption may not be present, potentially weakening the case for neutral gas around this object. Larger samples of z > 7 quasars may therefore be needed to establish a clearer picture of the IGM neutral fraction at these redshifts.

sources.summary: JWST/MRS observations of J1120+0641 detect hot torus dust and broad hydrogen lines, yielding a 1.52e9 solar-mass black hole and BLR properties comparable to lower-redshift quasars.

quotes.quote: We present a JWST/MRS spectrum of the quasar J1120+0641 at z=7.0848 , the first spectroscopic observation of a reionisation-era quasar in the rest-frame infrared wavelengths ( 0.6 < _ rest < 3.4 m). In the context of the mysterious fast assembly of the first supermassive black holes at z>7 , our observations enable for the first time the detection of hot torus dust, the H emission line, and the Paschen-series broad emission lines in a quasar at z>7 , which we compare to samples at z<6 for signs of evolution. Hot torus dust is clearly detected as an upturn in the continuum emission at _ rest 1.3 m, leading to a black-body temperature of T_ dust = 1413.5_ 7.4 ^ +5.7 K. Compared to similarly-luminous quasars at 0<z<6 , the hot dust in J1120+0641 is somewhat elevated in temperature (top 1 ).

We measure the black hole mass of J1120+0641 based on the H Balmer line, M_ BH = 1.52 0.17 10^9 M_ , which is in good agreement with the previous rest-UV Mg II black hole mass measurement. The black hole mass based on the Paschen-series lines is also consistent within uncertainties, indicating that no significant extinction is biasing the M_ BH measurement obtained from the rest-frame UV. By comparing the ratios of the H , Pa- and Pa- emission lines to predictions from a simple one-phase Cloudy model, we find that the hydrogen broad lines are consistent with originating in a common broad-line region (BLR) with density log N_H/ cm ^ 3 12 , ionisation parameter 7< log \ U<-4 , and extinction E(B--V) 0.1 mag. These BLR parameters are fully consistent with similarly-bright quasars at 0<z<4 .

sources.summary: The paper uses HST imaging of ULAS J1120+0641, a z=7.0842 quasar, to study its environment, near-zone opacity, massive black hole context, and lack of excess bright neighboring galaxies.

quotes.quote: We present optical and near-infrared imaging of the field of the z=7.0842 quasar ULAS J112001.48+064124.3 taken with the Hubble Space Telescope . We use these data to search for galaxies that may be physically associated with the quasar, using the Lyman break technique, and find three such objects, although the detection of one in Spitzer Space Telescope imaging strongly suggests it lies at z 2 . This is consistent with the field luminosity function and indicates that there is no excess of >L^* galaxies within 1 Mpc of the quasar.

The quasar ULAS J112001.48+064124.3 (hereafter ULAS J1120+0641; Mortlock et al. \ 2011) provided a new method of measuring the neutrality of the IGM. Discovered with a combination of infrared imaging from the UKIDSS Large Area Survey (Lawrence et al. \ 2007) and optical imaging from the Sloan Digital Sky Survey (SDSS; Abazajian et al. \ 2009), augmented with deeper imaging from the Liverpool Telescope, its redshift was originally measured as z=7.085 0.003 from the broad ultraviolet emission lines, later refined to z=7.0842 from a detection of the [ C  ii ]   158 m line by Venemans et al. \ (2012).

ULAS J1120+0641 is therefore the first quasar to be discovered within the Epoch of Reionization. The environment of ULAS J1120+0641 may therefore provide an opportunity to study galaxy formation and evolution in a currently unique environment, since distant active galaxies have often been successfully used as signposts to locate overdensities in the Universe.

sources.summary: The paper models the z=7.084 QSO ULAS J1120+0641 spectrum, focusing on seven transmission spikes and a 240 cMpc/h Gunn-Peterson trough that calibrated reionization simulations reproduce well.

quotes.quote: Recently, 2017A&A...601A..16B (B17 hereafter) obtained a very high S/N spectrum of the QSO with the highest redshift known, ULAS J1120+0641 at z=7.084 ( 2011Natur.474..616M ). B17 used Hubble Space Telescope (HST) imaging to flux-calibrate and combined additional data with their previous spectroscopic observations with the X-shooter instrument at the Very Large Telescope (VLT). They reported the detection of seven narrow transmission spikes in the redshift range 5.858<z<6.122 followed by the longest Gunn-Peterson trough ( 1965ApJ...142.1633G ) of complete absorption known in a QSO spectrum. The long trough extends from z=6.122 up to z=7.04 at the edge of the QSO near zone and corresponds to a length of 240 cMpc/ h .

Here we will model both the observed transmission spikes and the long trough in the spectrum of ULAS J1120+0641 with radiative transfer simulations that were originally introduced in 2015MNRAS.453.2943C ). Our main goal is to model the transmission spikes identified by B17 in the observed spectrum of ULAS J1120+0641 (see also 2006MNRAS.370.1401G for some modelling of the dependence of high-redshift transmission spikes on the reionization history based on a semi-analytical model of QSO absorption spectra).

Using our well calibrated simulations with a narrow range of three reionization histories (early, fiducial and late), we were able to reproduce the occurrence of seven transmission spikes, the observed integrated transmitted flux and the length of the totally absorbed GP trough in the observed spectrum of ULAS J1120+0641 very well.

sources.summary: The paper applies PCA continuum modeling to ULAS J1120+0641 at z=7.09, finding a template redshift near systemic, peculiar broad-line blueshifts, and nearest-neighbor spectra with similar red-side profiles.

quotes.quote: We now demonstrate our continuum fitting machinery on the two highest-redshift quasars known: ULAS J1120+0641 ( z=7.09 , Mortlock11 ) and ULAS J1342+0928 ( z=7.54 , Banados17 ). Modeling the intrinsic continuum of these quasars, and understanding the uncertainty in those models, is critical to constraining the neutral fraction of the IGM, so they provide a first test of the applicability of our continuum modeling procedure. For our analysis of the two z>7 quasars, we use the previously published spectra from their respective discovery papers. We use the combined VLT/FORS2 + Gemini/GNIRS spectrum of ULAS J1120+0641 published in Mortlock11 and we use the combined Magellan/FIRE + Gemini/GNIRS spectrum of ULAS J1342+0928 published in Banados17 .

For ULAS J1120+0641 we find z_ temp =7.0834 , a very small blueshift of v=63 km/s from the systemic redshift ( z_ sys =7.0851 from host galaxy [ C 2 ] emission, Venemans17 ), while for ULAS J1342+0928 we find z_ temp =7.4438 , a blueshift of v=3422 km/s from the systemic redshift ( z_ sys =7.5413 from host galaxy [ C 2 ] emission, Venemans17b ). Both of these quasars have peculiar broad emission line properties, most notably large blueshifts in the C 4 line relative to Mg 2 : v 2800 km/s for ULAS J1120+0641, and v 6100 km/s for ULAS J1342+0928.

While the ULAS J1120+0641 neighbors have strikingly similar red-side broad emission line profiles, the ULAS J1342+0928 neighbors are less similar, reflecting the more sparse sampling of the distribution of quasar spectra.

sources.summary: The paper applies QSANNdRA to ULAS J1120+0641, reconstructs its z>7 quasar spectrum, and uses it to infer the IGM neutral fraction at z=7.0851.

quotes.quote: In comparison with the extended PCA model, QSANNdRA further achieves an improvement of 22.1 and 16.8 when evaluated on low-redshift quasars most similar to the two high-redshift quasars under consideration, ULAS J1120+0641 at z=7.0851 and ULAS J1342+0928 at z=7.5413 , respectively. Using our more accurate reconstructions of these two z>7 quasars, we estimate the neutral fraction of the IGM using a homogeneous reionization model and find x _ H 1 = 0.25^ +0.05 _ 0.05 at z=7.0851 and x _ H 1 = 0.60^ +0.11 _ 0.11 at z=7.5413 .

In particular, we aim to apply our model to the combined VLT/FORS + Gemini/GNIRS spectrum of ULAS J1120+0641 2011Natur.474..616M and the Magellan/FIRE + Gemini/GNIRS spectrum of ULAS J1342+0928 2018Natur.553..473B . Applying the trained autoencoder to ULAS J1120+0641 and ULAS J1342+0928 and calculating their corresponding mean squared errors revealed that they fall onto the 48th percentile and the 99th percentile in our low-redshift distribution (see figure fig:hist ). While ULAS J1120+0641 seems to be well represented in our low-redshift dataset, ULAS J1342+0928 falls onto the tail-end of our distribution. In particular, on the 100 quasars most similar to ULAS J1120+0641 (at z=7.085 , shown in orange), we achieve a 22.1 improvement in (left) and a 26.2 improvement in _ (right). Given that we are now confident that our model generalizes to high-redshift objects and performs better than other models in the literature, in this section, we apply QSANNdRA to two high-redshift quasars, namely ULAS J1120+0641 at z=7.0851 2011Natur.474..616M,2017ApJ...837..146V , and ULAS J1342+0928 at z=7.5413 2018Natur.553..473B to reconstruct their blue-side spectra and constrain the neutral gas fraction at their corresponding redshifts.

sources.summary: Chandra and XMM-Newton data detect the z=7.084 quasar, measure its X-ray luminosity and spectral slope, and suggest soft X-rays consistent with super-Eddington accretion.

quotes.quote: We present X-ray imaging and spectroscopy of the redshift z=7.084 radio-quiet quasar ULAS J112001.48+064124.3 obtained with Chandra and XMM-Newton . The quasar is detected as a point source with both observatories. The Chandra observation provides a precise position, confirming the association of the X-ray source and the quasar, while a sufficient number of photons is detected in the XMM-Newton observation to yield a meaningful X-ray spectrum.

In the XMM-Newton observation the quasar has a 2-10 keV luminosity of 4.7 0.9 \ 10^ 44 erg s ^ 1 and a spectral slope = 1.6^ +0.4 _ 0.3 (where f_ ^ ). The quasar appears to have dimmed in the 15 months between the two observations, with a 2-10 keV luminosity of 1.8^ +1.0 _ 0.7 10^ 45 erg s ^ 1 during the Chandra observation. We derive optical to X-ray spectral slopes _ OX of 1.76 0.07 and 1.54^ +0.09 _ 0.08 at the times of the XMM-Newton and Chandra observations respectively, consistent with the range of _ OX found in other quasars of comparable ultraviolet luminosity.

sources.summary: The paper presents a deep XMM-Newton X-ray spectrum of the z=7.085 quasar, finding normal photon index, luminosity, and X-ray-to-optical properties for optically bright quasars.

quotes.quote: We study the X-ray spectrum of ULAS1120+0641, the highest redshift quasar detected so far at z=7.085, which has been deeply observed (340 ks) by XMM-Newton. Despite the long integration time the spectral analysis is limited by the poor statistics, with only 150 source counts being detected. We measured the spectrum in the 2-80 keV rest-frame (0.3-10 keV observed) energy band.

Assuming a simple power law model we find a photon index of 2.0 0.3 and a luminosity of 6.7 0.3 10 ^ 44 erg s ^ 1 in the 2-10 keV band, while the intrinsic absorbing column can be only loosely constrained (N _ H < 10 ^ 23 cm ^ 2 ). Combining our measure with published data, we calculate that the X-ray-to-optical spectral index _ OX is1.8 0.1, in agreement with the _ OX UV luminosity correlation valid for lower redshift quasars. We expanded to high energies the coverage of the spectral energy distribution of ULAS1120+0641.

sources.summary: The paper treats ULAS J1120+0641 as a z=7.085 quasar whose unusually small near-zone and possible damping wing require a substantially neutral IGM in the simulations.

quotes.quote: This trend continues to the current highest redshift QSO, ULAS J1120+0641, at z=7.085 (see venemans2015,wu2015 and reed2015 for more recent updates with additional QSOs at z>6 ). The small near-zone size of ULAS J1120+0641, together with the possible discovery of a damping wing observed redward of Ly transmission, also suggests that the IGM at z 7 may be significantly more neutral than at z 6 mortlock2011,bolton2011 , but see bosman2015 for a reassessment of the significance of the evidence for suppressed emission in this QSO by a significantly neutral surrounding IGM.

mortlock2011 measure the near-zone of ULAS J1120+0641 to be 1.9 0.1 proper Mpc. This suggests that the near-zone of ULAS J1120+0641 is unlikely to be due to the proximity effect and may therefore be a useful probe of a substantially neutral IGM at z=7.085 . In the right panel of Figure tq_all , we compare the measured near-zone size of ULAS J1120+0641 (shaded purple region) to the simulations of the overdense region containing a 2.5 10^ 12 M_ halo. We find that only for simulations with a large average initial neutral fraction somewhere between f_ H i _ init = 0.1-1.0 can we reproduce the small observed near-zone size.

sources.summary: The paper uses ULASJ1120+0641 as a bright-QSO benchmark, adopting its ionizing photon luminosity to model an H I 21-cm search and estimate SKA1-low detection conditions.

quotes.quote: We also investigate the possibility of detecting HII regions around known bright QSOs such as around ULASJ1120+0641 discovered by mortlock11 . We find that a 5 detection is possible with 600 hr of SKA1-low observations if the QSO age and the outside 1 are at least 2 10^7 Myr and 0.2 respectively.

We assume that the quasar under consideration is emitting with a constant ionizing photon luminosity throughout its age and, therefore, growing in size following the model of (here we consider it to be same as the quasar ULASJ1120+0641 i.e. N _ = 1.3 10^ 57 sec^ 1 ). We consider the apparent anisotropy in the quasar regions shape arising due to the finite light travel time and include that as the matched filter parameter in our targeted search majumdar11,majumdar12 . The apparent radii of the ionized bubble for different QSO age and the outside 1 are shown in figure fig:bubble_size . We assume that the quasar is located at redshift z = 8 .

sources.summary: The paper finds irregular host features around J1120+0641, an offset quasar position, and differing near-IR and submillimeter emission regions consistent with merger-related complexity.

quotes.quote: J1120+0641 (z=7.08) was initially reported in mortlock11 . The PSF-subtracted images of J1120+0641 show irregular features, suggesting that the quasar might have experienced a recent galaxy merger. We also notice that the quasar is 0 5 away from the center of the host galaxy.

venemans17 reported the high-resolution ALMA observation of J1120+0641. The sub-mm continuum of J1120+0641 has a deconvolved FWHM of 1.24 kpc 0.83 kpc , which is much smaller than the near-IR emission as revealed by NIRCam imaging. This result indicates that the sub-mm emission and the rest-frame optical emission come from different regions in the quasar host galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper models GMRT 21-cm detectability of the ionized bubble around ULASJ1120+0641, estimating how neutral fraction and quasar age affect required observing times for 3- and 5-sigma detections.

quotes.quote: We first consider the possibility of detecting the bubble around the quasar ULASJ1120+0641 mortlock11 using GMRT redshifted 21-cm observation. The size and shape of the bubble depend on 1 and _Q , and we have determined the observation time that will be required for different values of these parameters. The left and right panels of Figure fig:mort show the observation time that will be required for a 3 and 5 detection respectively. We find that for 1,000 hr of observation a 3 detection is possible in a considerable region of parameter space where 1>0.4 and >1.5 , and a 5 detection is possible in a relatively smaller region of parameter space where 1>0.7 and >3.5 . A 3 detection is possible for a considerably large region of the parameter space ( 1>0.2 and >1.0 ) with 4,000 hr of observation, and we require approximately 8,000 hr of observation for a 5 detection in this region of parameter space.

sources.summary: The paper uses ULAS J1120 + 0641 as the high-redshift quasar benchmark whose red damping wing constrains IGM opacity near reionization.

quotes.quote: In this paper, we shall argue that one other clue to this puzzle is provided by the quasar ULAS J1120 + 0641 at z=7.085 , recently discovered by the UKIRT Infrared Deep Sky Survey (UKIDSS, Lawrence07 ). The spectrum of ULAS J1120 + 0641 exhibits a strong damping wing extending redward of the quasar emission line ( Mortlock11 ) -- exactly what is needed to suppress the generally redshifted (relative to the systemic redshift of the galaxy) emission of LAEs. Theoretical models predict that the regions surrounding rare, bright quasars are amongst the first to reionise (e.g. Furlanetto04b,Iliev06b,TracCen07,Zahn07,McQuinn07 ).

For comparison, the grey binned line in all panels displays the transmission profile of the z=7.085 quasar ULAS J1120 + 0641 ( Mortlock11 ); a strong damping wing extends redwards of systemic despite the expected quasar proximity effect and the fact that the biased regions where bright quasars form should reionise earlier than on average. This implies that if similar ionisation conditions hold around bright LAEs at z=7 , an even stronger red damping wing may result.

sources.summary: The paper uses ULAS J1120+0641, a z=7.085 quasar, as the highest-redshift anchor in its quasar Hubble diagram, noting its inferred luminosity distance matches the best-fit curves.

quotes.quote: All 17 of these sources, together with several others from ref. , and the newest quasar ULAS J1120+0641 at z=7.085 , are included in Table 1 below.

We notice, in particular, that ULAS J1120+0641 (at z=7.085 ) fits the theoretical curves remarkably well, confirming our suspicion that quasars tend to accrete at a rate closer to the Eddington value the higher their redshift. The importance of this source in anchoring the fits shown in figure 1 is quite evident, for it provides a significant stretching in the range of sampled redshifts.

In this regard, the work we have reported here would not have been feasible without the recent discovery of ULAS J1120+0641 at z=7.085 . Quite remarkably, the inferred luminosity distance to this source matches the best-fit curves rather well.

sources.summary: The simulations explain ULAS J1120+0641 as a high-redshift quasar grown from a direct-collapse black hole fed by cold streams, matching its mass, luminosity, host properties, and emission-region mass.

quotes.quote: But the recent discoveries of ULAS J1120+0641, a 2 10^9 \ BH at z = 7.1, and ULAS J1342+0928, a 8.0 10^ 8 \ BH at z = 7.5, now push the era of quasar formation up to just 690 Myr after the Big Bang. Here we report new cosmological simulations of SMBHs with X-rays fully coupled to primordial chemistry and hydrodynamics that show that J1120 and J1342 can form from direct collapse black holes (DCBHs) if their growth is fed by cold, dense accretion streams, like those thought to fuel rapid star formation in some galaxies at later epochs. Our models reproduce all of the observed properties of J1120: its mass, luminosity, and H II region as well as star formation rates and metallicities in its host galaxy. They also reproduce the dynamical mass of the innermost 1.5 kpc of its emission region recently measured by ALMA and J-band magnitudes that are in good agreement with those found by the VISTA Hemisphere Survey.

While our numerical simulations show that J1120 can be explained as a DCBH that formed at the intersection of cold accretion streams at z 20, they also reveal that J1342 could have formed in the same way.

sources.summary: The paper models ULAS J1120+0641 as a z=7.09 quasar with possible damping-wing absorption, a small proximity zone, and weak constraints on a substantially neutral IGM.

quotes.quote: In Figure fig:mortlock_pca , we show the red-side fit to the VLT/FORS2 + Gemini/GNIRS spectrum of ULAS J1120+0641 ( Mortlock11 ; top panel) and the predicted blue-side continuum (bottom panel) from Paper I. The predicted continuum has been corrected for the mean bias of predicted continua for similar quasars in the training set, as discussed in sec:pca and shown in Figure 12 of Paper I. We find a best-fit template redshift of z=7.0834 , a very small blueshift of v=63 km/s from the systemic frame defined by the centroid of the [ CII ] emission line of the host galaxy ( z=7.0851 , Venemans17 ). The blue-side profile shows a hint of absorption redward of Ly and a relatively small proximity zone ( R_ p =1.72 proper Mpc, following the definition in Eilers17 ) compared to the trend seen at z 5.7 - 6.5 Eilers17,Mazzucchelli17 .

While our analysis of ULAS J1120+0641 suggests a neutral fraction of x_ HI 0.5 , the posterior PDF is not particularly constraining, with significant probability density at the x_ HI =0 and x_ HI =1 boundaries.

sources.summary: The appendix says this faint high-redshift quasar had a line in an X-SHOOTER order gap, required iron-template assumptions, yet allowed another line and complex to be modeled.

quotes.quote: ULAS J1120+0641 Unfortunately, the line of this spectrum falls in the gap between the last two orders of the X-SHOOTER spectrograph. Due to the faint nature of the quasar the extracted traces of the last orders do not overlap and strong artifacts plague the echelle order boundary. Hence, we were unable to fit the line. To properly fit the continuum, including the iron contribution, we have assumed a FWHM for the iron template of FWHM _ FeII =2500 km s ^ 1 and set the redshift to the systemic redshift of the quasar. The high redshift of this quasar allows us to successfully model the line (single Gaussian component) as well as the complex.

sources.summary: The paper searches for the ULAS J112001.48+064124.3 host, finding no NIRCam band detections and arguing its dynamical mass and low extinction disfavor a hidden massive compact host.

quotes.quote: The final two quasars, SDSS J1148+5251 and ULAS J112001.48+064124.3 (J1120+0641) at z 6.4 and 7.1 respectively, are among the highest-redshift quasars known, and we obtained only NIRCam images to search for hosts via PSF subtraction. However, for J1120+0641 in particular, its small dynamical mass of (4.3 0.9) 10^ 10 M _ Venemans2017 , even considering the small sub-mm size of the continuum and line source, argues persuasively against the possibility of a very massive compact host. J1148+5251 and J1120+0641 (bottom two rows, the two highest-redshift quasars in our sample) are undetected in all bands.

For the galaxies with an F360M flux (J2239+0207, J1148+5251, and J1120+0641), we utilize that flux to calculate the mass. Assuming that the two quasars in our sample without large far-infrared excesses lie in relatively typical z 6 galaxies, these low extinction levels support our assumption that the galaxy-scale interstellar extinction is low in J0731+4459 and J1120+0641.

sources.summary: The paper describes ULAS J1120+0641 as a z=7.085 quasar whose GP trough indicates a smaller ionized region and possible red-side damping-wing absorption.

quotes.quote: In particular, fully black Gunn-Peterson (GP) troughs GP1965 , have been found in a number of z > 6 Sloan Digital Sky Survey (SDSS) quasars, as well as in the quasar ULAS J1120+0641 recently discovered at z=7.085 Mortlock+11 in the UKIDDS survey UKIDDS . The presence of these troughs suggests that these sources may be revealing the tail--end of reionization (see, e.g., the review by FCK06 ).

In the case of the UKIDDS quasar ULAS J1120+0641, the GP trough is located at a wavelength much closer to the Ly line center, i.e. the ionized region appears markedly smaller. As a result, the situation for this object is somewhat different from the SDSS quasars: the red GP damping wing can cause significant (10-20 ) absorption on the red side of the Ly line Bolton+11 .

sources.summary: The paper presents ULAS J1120+0641 as a Bayesian-selected UKIDSS discovery, then the most distant known quasar and a major early-universe follow-up target.

quotes.quote: The new discoveries included ULAS J1120+0641 Mortlocketal:2011d , which is currently the most distant quasar known. It is seen as it was just 0.75 billion years after the Big Bang. As the only bright source known that early in the history of the Universe, ULAS J1120+0641 is the target of a large number of follow-up science observations, including by the Hubble Space Telescope . ULAS J1120+0641 is currently one of the most important astronomical sources known, and Bayesian methods were critical to its discovery.

sources.summary: The review highlights ULAS J1120+0641 as the most distant record-holding z>6 quasar, with an actively accreting massive black hole and lower luminosity than J0100.

quotes.quote: The two noteworthy record holders are ULAS J1120+0641 (J1120) and SDSS J0100+2802 (J0100), hosting the most distant ( z 7.1 , Mortlock11 ), and the most massive ( 1.2 10^ 10 M_ , Wu15 ) SMBHs ever observed respectively. In the right panel of Fig. fig:fig1 we show the bolometric luminosity as a function of the BH mass for the collection of z 6 quasars presented by Wu15 . The nuclei of these objects are actively accreting massive BHs, shining close to or above the Eddington luminosity (green dashed line). Colored points show three of the most interesting objects observed to date: the two record holders introduced above, J1120 (magenta triangle) and J0100 (blue square) and quasar SDSS J1148+5251 (red circle, hereafter J1148) which is one of the best studied quasar, discovered at z=6.4 Fan01 . As it can be seen from the figure, J0100 is the most luminous quasar known at z>6 , with bolometric luminosity L_ Bol =L_ Edd 4 10^ 14 L _ Wu15 , making it 4 times brighter than J1148 (red circle), and 7 times brighter than J1120 (magenta triangle).

sources.summary: The paper describes ULAS J1120+0641 as a z>7 quasar discovered in near-infrared survey data, formerly the redshift record holder at z=7.09 and one of only two known z>7 quasars.

quotes.quote: Being observed only one billion years after the Big Bang, z 7 quasars are a unique opportunity for exploring the early Universe. However, only two z 7 quasars have been discovered in near-infrared surveys: the quasars ULAS J1120+0641 and ULAS J1342+0928 at z = 7.09 and z = 7.54 , respectively.

The use of such data allowed Mortlock2011 to discover the previous redshift record holder, ULAS J1120+0641 at z=7.09 , before it was superceded by ULAS J1342+0928 at z=7.54, for which Banados2018 mined data from the UKIDSS Large Area Survey Lawrence2007 , the Wide-field Infrared Survey Explorer ALLWISE, Wright2010 , and the DECam Legacy Survey (DECaLS http://legacysurvey.org/decamls/ ). However, despite all these efforts, these two quasars are the only ones found at redshift z > 7 to date.

sources.summary: The paper treats ULASJ1120+0641 as a z=7.1 QSO whose spectra provide the first detection of ongoing reionization and strong constraints on the neutral fraction.

quotes.quote: The strongest constraints come from the optical depth to the CMB measured with the Planck satellite and the first detection of ongoing reionization from the spectra of the z=7.1 QSOs ULASJ1120+0641.

The strongest constraints here come from the first detection of ongoing reionization , obtained from the spectra of the z=7.1 QSOs ULASJ1120+0641: (z=7.1) = 0.4^ +0.41 _ 0.32 (2- ); see also the recent work by who obtain comparable limits from the disappearance of Lyman alpha emitting galaxies beyond z 6 (not shown in the figure).
The strongest constraints come from the optical depth to the CMB, and the first detection of ongoing reionization from the spectra of the z=7.1 QSOs ULASJ1120+0641.

sources.summary: The paper uses ULAS J1120+0641's Ly absorption to infer ionizing output and radiative efficiency, noting its truncated proximity zone, damping wing evidence, and low inferred efficiency.

quotes.quote: The two highest redshift quasars known, ULAS J1120+0641 Mortlock11 (henceforth J1120+0641) at z=7.09 , and ULAS J1342+0928 Banados18 (henceforth J1342+0928) at z=7.54 , both exhibit truncated proximity zones Bolton11,Davies18b (compared to similarly-luminous quasars at z 6 - 6.5 , Eilers17 ) and show strong evidence for damping wing absorption Mortlock11,Bolton11,Greig17b,Banados18,Davies18b .

For J1120+0641 and J1342+0928, we compute ionizing photon emission rates of N _ ion ^ J1120 =1.2 10^ 57 s ^ 1 and N _ ion ^ J1342 =1.4 10^ 57 s ^ 1 , and bolometric luminosities of L_ bol ^ J1120 =2.7 10^ 13 L_ and L_ bol ^ J1342 =3.1 10^ 13 L_ , respectively.
The posterior median radiative efficiencies of J1120+0641 and J1342+0928 are 0.08 and 0.1 , respectively, and the canonical 10 is ruled out at greater than 98 per cent probability by each quasar.

sources.summary: The paper includes J1120+0641 as a z=7.09 quasar observed in Chandra Cycle 12, processed in Faint telemetry mode, and detected in X-rays by wavdetect.

quotes.quote: Specifically, J112001.48+064124.3 hereafter J1120+0641, Mortlock11 at z=7.09 was observed in Cycle 12 Page14 , J134208.10 +092838.6 hereafter J1342+0928, Banados18b at z=7.54 was observed in Cycle 18 Banados18a , and four other quasars were observed in Cycle 19 Vito19 .
J1120+0641 was observed with the Faint telemetry format and all other quasars were observed with the Very Faint mode.

sources.summary: The paper identifies ULAS J1120+0641 as the highest-redshift quasar in its sample and the first known z>7 quasar, using combined Magellan/FIRE and VLT/X-Shooter spectra.

quotes.quote: For three of the quasars in our sample, i.e. J1030+0524, J159--02, and J1120+0641, we use combined Magellan/FIRE and VLT/X-Shooter vernet_x-shooter_2011 spectra, while one other spectrum in our sample, i.e. J1148+5251, was observed with Keck/MOSFIRE mclean_design_2010,mclean_mosfire_2012 and Keck/ESI sheinis_esi_2002 .

Note that the highest-redshift quasar in our sample is ULAS J1120+0641, which has been studied extensively e.g. simcoe_extremely_2012,bosman_deep_2017,schindler_x-shooteralma_2020 as it was the first z>7 quasar ever discovered mortlock_luminous_2011 .

The spectrum of ULAS J1120+0641 that we are using in this paper has been observed with Magellan/FIRE and VLT/X-Shooter, as compared to the VLT/FORS and Gemini/GNIRS spectrum that has been originally published for this object.

gmw_00001752.name: J0650+6001
gmw_00001752.overview: J0650+6001 is a radio-loud quasar located at a redshift of z = 0.455 [arXiv:1111.1185]. Its three-component radio structure exhibits asymmetric outer regions [arXiv:1111.1185], core variability [arXiv:1111.1185], and potential high-energy gamma-ray emission [arXiv:1510.06763].

sources.summary: The paper describes J0650+6001 as a z=0.455 radio quasar with a three-component radio structure, asymmetric outer regions, measurable hotspot separation, and possible high-energy inverse-Compton emission.

quotes.quote: The radio source J0650+6001 is associated with a quasar at redshift z =0.455. The source is resolved into three components and its radio luminosity is 10 ^ 46 erg/s. The central component shows a flat spectrum, suggesting the presence of the core, while the two outer regions, with a steeper spectral index, display a highly asymmetric flux density (Fig. j0650 ). Multi-epoch analysis of the changes in the pc-scale structure shows that the outer components are separating with a velocity of (0.39 0.19), which corresponds to a kinematic age of 360 170 years.

Furthermore, the separation between the core component and the southern hotspot seems to contract with an apparent velocity of (0.37 0.02). Such contraction is interpreted in terms of a mildly relativistic knot in the jet, still embedded in the central component, that is moving towards the southern component mo10 . This interpretation is supported by the detection of some variability related to the central component, as pointed up by the 5-GHz light curve mo10 .

sources.summary: The paper flags J0650+6001 as a CSO gamma-ray candidate, computes its expected 1-GeV flux, and notes that moderate boosting in an SSRQ-associated CSO could add inverse-Compton emission.

quotes.quote: Interestingly, in the core luminosity vs total luminosity plot 3 CSO lie in the region occupied by blazars in the Fermi LAT Bright AGN Sample (LBAS; Abdo et al. 2009c): OQ 208, J0650 + 6001, and PKS 1413 + 135, making these CSO as good candidates for high-energy emission.

By means of Equations lum and flux , we computed the expected 1-GeV luminosity and flux density for OQ 208 and J0650+6001, which turn out to be: OQ 208: L _ 1 GeV = 2.8 10 ^ 44 erg s ^ 1 , S _ 1 GeV = 3.8 10 ^ 11 erg cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 ; J0650+6001: L _ 1 GeV = 5 10 ^ 44 erg s ^ 1 , S _ 1 GeV = 1.2 10 ^ 12 erg cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 .

However, in the case of CSO associated with SSRQ, like J0650+6001, where also moderate boosting effects should be present, we may consider an additional contribution of IC made by relativistic electrons from the jet enhanced by boosting effects (e.g., Ghisellini et al. 2005).

gmw_00001753.name: J0912+1523
gmw_00001753.overview: J0912+1523 is a massive, recently quenched post-starburst galaxy at a redshift of z = 0.747 [arXiv:1804.10216]. It features a spheroidal component embedded within a rotating disk [arXiv:2111.14878], exhibits strong ordered motion [arXiv:2010.04734, arXiv:1804.10216], and has nearby companion galaxies that may be physically associated [arXiv:2111.14878].

sources.summary: The paper describes J0912+1523 as a massive post-starburst galaxy with strong ordered motion, a slightly increasing H-delta profile, and constraints inconsistent with an unresolved secondary starburst.

quotes.quote: Two galaxies (J0912+1523 and J0835+3121) show clear signs of strong ordered motion, with a third (J1109-0040) showing weaker but still significant velocity gradient. The other three galaxies do not exhibit statistically significant velocity gradients. Detailed analysis of the intrinsic velocity structures of these galaxies would require a more precise model of the point spread function and is outside the scope of the current paper (see Hunt2018 for more in depth discussion about the velocity structure of J0912+1523).

Four of the six galaxies are consistent with a flat H _A gradient at the 2- level, while the galaxy J0912+1523 has a slightly increasing H _A profile. Only J0835+3121 exhibits a negative gradient in H _A .

The exact constraints vary between the galaxies, with extreme cases like J0912+1523 being entirely inconsistent with an unresolved secondary starburst. Other galaxies are less constrained due to less extended profiles or worse seeing conditions, but they still can only produce the observed profiles if the central burst is comparable in mass to any underlying population.

sources.summary: The paper studies SDSS J0912+1523 as a massive z=0.747 post-starburst galaxy with quenched star formation, strong rotation, large cold-gas fraction, and aligned stellar and gas kinematics.

quotes.quote: In this Letter, we examine an intermediate-redshift PSB, SDSS J0912+1523, with a stellar mass of  2 10 ^ 11  M_ . The target was chosen out of a large sample of PSBs suess17 selected from the SDSS DR12 catalog alam15 and included in pete16 . The galaxies in the PSB sample were identified by their strong Balmer breaks and blue slopes redward of the break, as demonstrated in kriek10 . SDSS J0912+1523 was chosen as the brightest, most A-star dominated source at the high end of the sample's redshift range, with z = 0.747 and i _ AB  = 18.6 mag. SDSS J0912+1523 represents a rare opportunity to study the spatially resolved kinematics of a massive, recently-quenched PSB.

Through kinematic and spectroscopic analysis of SDSS J0912+1523 using optical IFU data obtained with Gemini, in conjunction with the analysis of ALMA data first presented in suess17 , we determine that the massive z 0.7 galaxy: is a young PSB that has effectively quenched its star formation; may contain either two cores or a dust lane that bisects a single core; has no detectable age gradient within 5 kpc of its center, within the constraints of the seeing; maintains significant rotational support compared to similar-mass quiescent galaxies; contains an unusually large cold gas fraction for its apparent SFR, and maintains ordered gas rotation that roughly matches the stellar component, with no evidence of significant outflow.

sources.summary: The paper presents J0912+1523 as a recently quenched target with nearby possible companions, a spheroidal component embedded in a disk, and rotating-disk kinematics from IFU follow-up.

quotes.quote: These galaxies are clearly disturbed: J2202-0033 has a large tidal feature to the west, and both J0027+0129 and J0912+1523 have nearby companions which may be physically associated. The image of J0912+1523 also reveals that it has a spheroidal component embedded within a disk.

The bright, recently-quenched galaxies provide an ideal testbed for IFU studies to directly probe the kinematics of galaxies just after quenching. An early result for one target (J0912+1523, also shown in Figure fig:hst ) revealed that the galaxy is a rotating disk hunt18 .

gmw_00001754.name: J0835+3121
gmw_00001754.overview: J0835+3121 is a massive post-starburst galaxy that exhibits clear signs of strong ordered motion [arXiv:2010.04734]. It features a negative H-delta absorption gradient and a lower preferred central-burst stellar fraction than other sample galaxies [arXiv:2010.04734].

sources.summary: The paper describes J0835+3121 as a massive post-starburst galaxy with ordered motion, the strongest negative H-delta gradient, and a lower preferred central-burst fraction than the other targets.

quotes.quote: Two galaxies (J0912+1523 and J0835+3121) show clear signs of strong ordered motion, with a third (J1109-0040) showing weaker but still significant velocity gradient. The other three galaxies do not exhibit statistically significant velocity gradients.
If we remove J0835+3121, which is host to the most significant gradient, there is a small signature of stronger absorption at large radii.

In Figure fig:4449_model , we show the results of the fitting for for J0835+3121, which prefers a lower secondary burst fraction in contrast to the other 5 galaxies in our sample. For this galaxy, we place a rough upper limit on the fraction of stars that formed in a central burst: above this fraction, we would expect our observed profile to be significantly more flat than the observed profile.

gmw_00001755.name: J1109-0040
gmw_00001755.overview: J1109-0040 is a massive post-starburst galaxy [arXiv:2010.04734]. It exhibits a weak velocity gradient and a flat H-alpha profile that requires a large central burst fraction in modeling [arXiv:2010.04734].

sources.summary: The paper describes J1109-0040 as a massive post-starburst galaxy with a weaker but significant velocity gradient and a flat profile requiring a large central burst fraction in the model.

quotes.quote: Two galaxies (J0912+1523 and J0835+3121) show clear signs of strong ordered motion, with a third (J1109-0040) showing weaker but still significant velocity gradient. The other three galaxies do not exhibit statistically significant velocity gradients.

In Figure fig:0278_model , we show the results of the fitting on one of the galaxies with a flat H _A profile, J1109-0040. The left panel shows the corner plot for the 3 model parameters with 68-, 95-, and 99-percent contours bounding the data. The right panel shows the observed H _A profile along with the median and 1- bounds in the best fitting model generated from 1000 random draws from the posterior distributions.

The posteriors for the four remaining galaxies are shown in Figure fig:all_corner . All four of them are similar to J1109-0040 in that the burst fraction can be constrained as a single tailed distribution from a 100 burst, which will result in a perfectly flat profile.

gmw_00001756.name: J0013+5119
gmw_00001756.overview: J0013+5119 is a doubly imaged quasar located at a redshift of z=2.63 [arXiv:2404.09954]. Radio emission has been detected from both the lens galaxy and the two lensed quasar images, which are separated by 2.92 arcseconds [arXiv:2404.09954].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J0013+5119 as a z=2.63 doubly imaged quasar with VLA radio emission from the lens and both quasar images, but a simple lens model failed.

quotes.quote: J0013+5119 was discovered as a doubly imaged quasar by Lemon19 using the Wide-field Infrared Survey Explorer (WISE) and Gaia DR2 catalogues. The quasar source is at redshift z=2.63 and the two images are separated by 2''.92 . Our VLA A-config observations revealed radio emission from both the lens and the quasar images, which are all fit to point sources in X-band using the CASA task imfit but appear as one source in VLASS.

The flux densities for the lensed images A and B taken from imfit give a flux ratio A/B = 1.0 0.2 , similar to the optical flux ratio of 1.2 based on the Gaia g band measurements. The lens galaxy was not detected in Gaia and was too blended with quasar light to get a precise position measurement in any other available optical survey, so our probability consideration only includes the two measured quasar positions. The system probability of random coincidence is then 3 10^ 11 .

However, when testing our best-fit results from this method, we found the source plane positions of images A and B did not match, i.e. the model was not accurately reproducing observations. We suspect this is due to the environment of the lens, and examining wider-field survey images of the J0013+5119 system show other galaxies of similar redshift in the vicinity of the lens, which could lead to a more complex lens model.

gmw_00001757.name: J2145+6345
gmw_00001757.overview: J2145+6345 is a bright, strongly lensed quasar located at a redshift of z=1.56 [arXiv:2404.09954]. The system features four optically bright images in a configuration similar to PG1115+080, though the lensing galaxy itself remains undetected [arXiv:1810.04480, arXiv:2404.09954].

sources.summary: The paper describes J2145+6345 as a bright quad in a crowded field with no detected lensing galaxy, suitable for time-delay monitoring and modeled with SIE+shear magnification.

quotes.quote: J2145+6345 This object was discovered by our W1 W2>0.2, 4 detections search. Three spectra were extracted for this object, since components A and B could not be resolved. It has a similar configuration and brightness to PG1115+080 weymann1980 .

No galaxy is revealed in the PSF subtraction of the Pan-STARRS data. The system is very bright in WISE (W1=12.08 in Vega) and corresponds to a faint ROSAT source voges1999 with 7 ROSAT photons within 30 arcseconds of the lens. Furthermore, all images are optically bright ( G =16.86, 17.26, 18.34 and 18.56), making it second only to PSJ0147+4630 berghea2017 in terms of the brightest fourth image. This makes the system ideal for high-cadence, high signal-to-noise monitoring to determine time delays for cosmography courbin2018 . Our mass model (SIE+shear) is constrained exclusively by image positions and fluxes, as the lensing galaxy is not detected.

sources.summary: The paper describes J2145+6345 as a z=1.56 quad lens suitable for time-delay studies, with three bright X-band images and radio flux ratios matching Gaia ratios.

quotes.quote: J2145+6345 was also discovered by Lemon19 using the same method as J0013+5100, and was singled out by the authors as being ideal for time-delay studies given its reasonably large image separation (a max of 2".07 ) and bright images. The quasar is located at z=1.56 , and Lemon19 report no detection of a lens galaxy in the PanSTARRS survey. We significantly detected the three brightest images of J2145+6345 in X-band as point sources, and also detected a noise bump coincident with the Gaia position of the fourth quasar image.

In VLASS, the system is blurred together into one component. Excluding the faintest image, which was not significantly detected, we obtained a system chance of random of 3 10^ 15 . We calculated the flux ratios between our significantly detected images as A/B = 1.7 0.2 and A/C = 3.3 0.5 . These radio flux ratios do not differ significantly from the Gaia g band flux ratios of A/B = 1.4 and A/C = 3.9 .

gmw_00001758.name: J2329-0734
gmw_00001758.overview: J2329-0734 is a strongly lensed quasar at a redshift of z = 1.31 [arXiv:1607.07476, arXiv:2404.09954]. It is a double-image lens system featuring a lensed host galaxy [arXiv:1607.07476], extended radio emission [arXiv:2404.09954], and a predicted time delay of 25 days [arXiv:2011.01971].

sources.summary: The paper detects WISE 2329 1258 in radio and optical images, finds a third radio component, and models the system with an added source-plane Gaussian component.

quotes.quote: WISE 2329 1258 WISE 2329 1258 is clearly detected in these observations, and has a previous detection in the NRAO-VLA Sky Survey with a flux density of 2.4 0.5 mJy at 1.4 GHz 1998AJ....115.1693C . The radio and optical image overlay in Fig. images is based on the Gaia astrometry, from which it appears that the brightest radio and optical components are coincident. Therefore, in principle, this object is a double-image lens system in which the lensed quasar images are visible both in the optical and radio. However, there is a clear detection of a third radio component, about 1 \ north, and slightly west, of the brightest component. A Keck adaptive-optics image of this source 2021MNRAS.503.1557S shows the two arcsecond-scale optical images of the background quasar, together with the lensing galaxy close to the fainter, north-eastern component.

We have used the separation between the bright image and the lensing galaxy from the Keck observation to set the position of the lensing galaxy in the radio image, assuming that the brightest points of the radio and optical emission are coincident. This has been adjusted by hand using a singular isothermal sphere model for the lensing galaxy until the brightest radio image is satisfactorily reproduced. An extra radio component is then added to the source plane, represented by a Gaussian in the source plane, and its properties, together with all parameters of the lensing galaxy except the position, are then allowed to vary.

sources.summary: The paper describes J2329-0734 as a z=1.31 lensed quasar with one bright X-band image and extended radio emission whose lens-model mapping suggests additional lensed structure.

quotes.quote: J2329-0734 was discovered by 2329disc , who used a WISE W1 - W2 color cut to select potential blended quasar pairs and crossmatched with the ATLAS survey. Candidates were checked for consistency in putative image colors and visually inspected before spectroscopic follow-up, which confirmed this object as a lensed quasar at z=1.31 . Our X-band observations detected the brighter image, as well as extended emission coming from just above that image. We detected a noise bump at the position of the other quasar image, but our image fitting procedure favored extended rather than point-source emission at this location.
