To further investigate the nature of this source, we turned to archival data from VLA project 19A-176, who obtained A-config observations of the object in the S and C bands. This data also shows pointlike features at the quasar image location and even more diffuse emission than the X-band data.
Image A's counterpart is located at image B, as expected, and the northeast component's counterimage is predicted to appear at the location of the southwest component.

sources.summary: The paper says WISE 2329-1258 has nearly identical quasar-image redshifts and constant flux ratio; later adaptive-optics data detected the lensing galaxy and lensed host galaxy.

quotes.quote: The two quasar images of WISE 2329-1258 have nearly identical redshifts with a rich set of absorption features present in both spectra, as shown in Figure 7. The MgII broad emission line is at z = 1.3077 0.0008 for the brighter component and z = 1.3174 0.0018 for the fainter component, overlapping at the 5 level. A similar agreement is seen for the CIII ] broad emission line.

The flux ratio between the components is also remarkably constant at about 2.5:1 (0.4 dex in Figure 7) over the entire IMACS spectral range. Based on the Magellan data presented here, WISE 2329-1258 would be classified as a lensless twin system. But Treu et al. (2017, private communication) have observed it using NIRC2 on Keck-II behind adaptive optics and detected the lensing galaxy as well as the lensed quasar host galaxy, so we count it as a confirmed lens.

sources.summary: The paper identifies WISE 2329--1258 as the noticeable-arc double lens with the longest predicted time delay, making it a possible but moderate-precision time-delay cosmography target.

quotes.quote: Although a number of doubles in our sample have noticeable lensed arcs, none of these systems have ideally long ( 100 days) time delays. Out of the systems with noticeable lensed arcs, WISE 2329--1258 has the longest predicted time-delay with 25 days. This is comparable to the time delay of PG 1115 + 080 with 8.4 per cent uncertainty on a single time-delay measurement, which has been analyzed to measure the Hubble constant Bonvin18, Chen18 . Since the modelling uncertainty for one double is also 8.5 per cent Birrer19, Wong20 , this system would lead to a 12--13 per cent Hubble constant measurement assuming a 3--5 per cent uncertainty coming from the external convergence estimate.

gmw_00001759.name: J000835.17-073405.6
gmw_00001759.overview: J000835.17-073405.6 is an optical quasar that was initially identified as a potential radio lens [arXiv:2404.09954]. However, follow-up radio observations detected only a single point source and no counterimage, suggesting the object is not a lensed system [arXiv:2404.09954].

sources.summary: The paper rejects J000835.17-073405.6 as a lens: X-band data find only one quasar point source, no counterimage, and implausible radio/optical flux-ratio behavior.

quotes.quote: This source was identified as a potential radio lens using the JB07 method, and was singled out for observation due to the relatively bright VLASS detection, green color of the potential source, and possible counterimage in DECaLS. However, our X-band follow-up revealed only a 2.37 mJy point source coincident with the optical quasar and no counterimage. Our VLA observations of this target have an rms noise of 25 Jy beam ^ 1 , and at the 5 level we should be sensitive to point sources brighter than 125 Jy. That we detect no radio counterimage suggest that if there were such a counterimage, the flux ratio of the lensed radio source would be a seemingly unrealistic >20 .

Moreover, the optical flux ratio of the sources immediately north-east and south-west of the the LRG is 8 , so should this be a lensed source then there would be a substantial discrepancy between the optical and radio flux ratios. While it is not impossible that this source is a lensed quasar, our observations don't support such a conclusion, and we posit that these are likely two unrelated sources.

gmw_00001760.name: J120157+421703
gmw_00001760.overview: J120157+421703 is a radio-loud quasar that was identified as a potential radio lens candidate [arXiv:2404.09954]. However, Very Large Array X-band observations revealed only a single 7.0 mJy point source coincident with the optical source and no counterimage [arXiv:2404.09954].

sources.summary: The paper treats J120157+421703 as a possible radio lens candidate but finds a single 7.0 mJy X-band point source and no counterimage.

quotes.quote: This source was identified as a possible radio lens using the JB07 method. The DECaLS image of this source shows a possible very faint arc to the lower right of the LRG. The VLA X-band data showed a 7.0 mJy point source coincident with the optical point source from DECaLS, but no counterimage. This presents two possibilities when taking the possible arc into account: either the arc is simply an image artifact or other phenomenon and there is no lensing present at all, or the quasar is at or near the lens redshift and is therefore not strongly lensed.

gmw_00001761.name: J135413+326937
gmw_00001761.overview: J135413+326937 is a radio-loud quasar that was initially identified as a potential radio lens candidate [arXiv:2404.09954]. However, X-band observations show only a single point source offset from the giant elliptical galaxy, indicating that the quasar is not multiply imaged [arXiv:2404.09954].

sources.summary: The paper discusses J135413+326937 as a potential radio lens candidate whose X-band observations show one offset quasar point source but no counterimage.

quotes.quote: J135413+326937 This source was identified as a potential radio lens using the JB07 method. The X-band observations show a 2.8 mJy point source offset from the LRG and coincident with the VLASS detection, but no counterimage. Therefore we conclude the quasar is not multiply imaged.

gmw_00001762.name: J233353+255450
gmw_00001762.overview: J233353+255450 is a luminous red galaxy that was initially identified as a possible radio lens candidate [arXiv:2404.09954]. However, re-imaged radio data revealed extended emission between the components, which is instead interpreted as a radio lobe from the galaxy [arXiv:2404.09954].

sources.summary: The paper discusses J233353+255450 as a possible radio lens candidate but interprets its re-imaged extended radio emission as an LRG radio lobe rather than lensing.

quotes.quote: J233353+255450 This source was identified as a possible radio lens using the JB07 method. Our initial X-band data reduction showed hints of extended emission near the lens location, and so we re-imaged the data with a 1'' uv-plane taper to increase sensitivity at the cost of resolution. We found an extended 1.7 mJy source located between the supposed lens and source, which we interpret as a radio lobe from the LRG rather than a lensed radio source, a hypothesis that is consistent with the steep spectrum ( =-0.8 ) we measure from the VLASS and X-band flux densities.

gmw_00001763.name: J171527+280452
gmw_00001763.overview: J171527+280452 is a possible radio lens candidate identified using the JB07 method [arXiv:2404.09954]. The source was not detected in X-band observations and shows no variability between VLASS epochs 1 and 2 [arXiv:2404.09954].

sources.summary: The paper discusses J171527+280452 as a possible radio lens candidate with no X-band detection, an inferred steep high-frequency spectral index, and unresolved lensing status.

quotes.quote: J171527+280452 This source was identified as a possible radio lens using the JB07 method. However, we detected no significant emission in our X-band observations. Based on the 2.6 mJy VLASS epoch 1 flux of the source and a 3 nondetection threshold, we estimate a spectral index between 3 and 10 GHz for this source of -2.2, much higher than its VLASS-NVSS spectral index of -0.31. The source shows no significant variability between VLASS epochs 1 and 2, leading us to suspect the target is either a peaked-spectrum compact source which is undetected at 10GHz, or an extended source which we do not detect due to resolution or sensitivity. In either case, we cannot rule out the possibility of lensing.

gmw_00001764.name: DES J0412 2646
gmw_00001764.overview: DES J0412 2646 is a gravitationally lensed galaxy identified using a neural network search [arXiv:2404.09954]. Radio observations reveal a point source at the lens galaxy location and a diffuse southern component, which may indicate a core-jet or core-lobe morphology [arXiv:2404.09954].

sources.summary: The paper describes DES J0412 2646 as a lensed galaxy with VLA emission at the lens location and diffuse southern emission, possibly from source-galaxy diffuse structure.

quotes.quote: DES J0412 2646 This source was identified as a lensed galaxy by Jacobs2019 using a Neural Network-based search of DES. While VLASS images from both epochs seem to be centered away from the lens, our follow-up data shows a 160 Jy point source at the location of the lens galaxy and a 260 Jy diffuse component to the south of that, possibly indicative of a core+jet or core+lobe morphology. Figure fig:0412 shows a DECaLS image of this source with the locations of our VLA detections and contours of two VLASS epochs.

While one epoch has the peak of emission located on top of the arc, the other places it between the arc and the lens galaxy. It is possible diffuse emission from the source galaxy is responsible for shifting the VLASS detection over, and that this emission is too low surface brightness for or resolved out of our observations at 10 GHz. However, further observations would be needed to address this hypothesis.

gmw_00001765.name: PMN J0630 24
gmw_00001765.overview: PMN J0630 24 is a high-power flat-spectrum radio quasar [arXiv:2310.03698] historically classified as a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:1203.4959, arXiv:2310.03698] at a redshift of approximately 1.238 [arXiv:1203.4959]. Its radio structure features an elongated core, a broad one-sided jet-like structure, and weaker emission on the opposite side [arXiv:0809.3100].

sources.summary: The paper models 5BZB J0630 2406 as a high-power FSRQ-like PeVatron blazar whose external radiation fields and energetic protons can yield IceCube-detectable neutrinos and hadronic X-ray signatures.

quotes.quote: Despite being historically classified as a BL Lac, our study shows that 5BZB J0630 2406 belongs to the relatively rare sub-class of high-power flat-spectrum radio quasars (FSRQs). Our results indicate that interactions between protons and external radiation fields can produce a neutrino flux that is within the reach of the IceCube detector. Furthermore, the spectral shape of the X-ray emission suggests the imprint of hadronic processes related to very energetic protons.

The relatively high accretion regime of 5BZB J0630-2406 is supported also by the ratio of the ray luminosity L_ 0.1 - 100 GeV and the Eddington luminosity, L_ / L_ Edd 0.15 . As further reprove of the intrinsic FSRQ nature, the model reveals an efficient accretion regime of 2 10^ 4 . Besides, a relatively large fraction of the ray luminosity ( 15 of L_ Edd ) is observed. Similarities can be found with the object TXS 0506+056 Padovani_2019 , where the localization of the emission region is thought to be, as in our case, on the edge of the BLR avoiding a significant absorption and an efficient neutrino production. The neutrino emission predicted within the lepto-hadronic framework is at reach of the IceCube detector, close to the flux sensitivity.

sources.summary: The paper presents spectroscopy of PMN J0630 24 as a bright BL Lac, detects multiple absorption features, and finds a redshift of about z=1.238.

quotes.quote: The BL Lacs PKS 0447 439 and PMN J0630 24 were observed at both the CTIO 4 m and NTT 3.6 m observatories and Table log gives the details of the observational set-up. Since any emission or absorption features in the spectra of these BL Lacs were expected to be weak, the observational strategy aimed at obtaining data of both a medium spectral resolution and a relatively high signal-to-noise ratio (S/N 50 ). The long-slit spectra were reduced using standard routines from the IRAF software package.

The absorption features are prominent and numerous in the spectra of the source PMN J0630 24, whereas only weak absorption features are detected in the spectra of the source PKS 0447 439. Based on the 2800 doublet in absorption, which is clearly detected in all spectra (see Fig. zoomspectra ), the redshift for the source PKS 0447 439 is z 1.246 and that of the source PMN J0630 24 is z 1.238 . The observed equivalent widths are W_ =0.3 and 1.4 for PKS 0447 439 and PMN J0630 24, respectively. Other absorption lines detected in the spectra of PMN J0630 24 are lines, most notably 2382 , 2586 and 2600 .

sources.summary: The paper identifies 3FGL J0630.9 2406 as the highest-redshift Fermi BL Lac in the sample, finds clear EBL absorption in its spectrum, and models it with unusually steep injection parameters.

quotes.quote: We select three Fermi detected fermi2fgl,fermi2lac sources, 3FGL J0022.1 1855 (J0022, z=0.774 ), 3FGL J0630.9 2406 (J0630, z>1.239 ), and 3FGL J0811.2 7529 (J0811, z=0.689 ), whose optical spectra are unusual, showing no emission lines but a set of strong low excitation (Mg I, Fe II, Al II etc) absorption lines on a blue, power-law continuum. These indicate that the AGN is viewed through the disk of an intervening absorber. In src+13 , this was taken to be the host galaxy; indeed for J0630 the photometry of rsg+12 supports this as the host redshift. With estimated redshifts of 0.774, > 1.239, and 0.689 rsg+12,src+13 for J0022, J0630 and J0811, respectively, these are thus luminous high-peak sources suitable for studying the extreme of the BL Lac population.

Not unexpectedly, EBL absorption provides no significant improvement to the fits of the lower redshift sources J0022 and J0811. However, we see clear improvements ( ^2 10 corresponding to 5 ) for J0630.
J0630 is the most extreme, with p_ 1 4.3 and a strong 1 G magnetic field.

sources.summary: The paper describes PMN J0630 24 as having an elongated northern core, one-sided diffuse radio emission, a broad jet-like structure, and weaker emission opposite the main structure.

quotes.quote: The VLA A array map (Fig. 7d) shows the core region to be elongated to the north. Most of the diffuse radio emission is observed perpendicular to the axis of the core elongation and extends to one side only. It has a broad jet-like structure. Some low-level extended radio emission is found to the opposite side of the large-scale extended radio structure.

gmw_00001766.name: PG 0934+013
gmw_00001766.overview: PG 0934+013 is a spiral host galaxy [arXiv:1710.00129] containing a disk with a bar and spiral arms [arXiv:2102.09190], and it is classified as a narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy [arXiv:1710.00129]. The galaxy exhibits star formation along its bar [arXiv:2206.13189], a biconical AGN-ionized zone [arXiv:2206.13189], and a compact radio core with a weak southern extension [arXiv:2301.07929].

sources.summary: The paper presents SALT reverberation mapping of the nearby Seyfert 1 galaxy PG 0934+013, measuring an H-beta lag, black-hole mass, host morphology, and scaling-relation consistency.

quotes.quote: We present the variability and time lag measurements of PG 0934+013 based on a photometric and spectroscopic monitoring campaign over a two year period. We obtained 46 epochs of data from the spectroscopic campaign, which was carried out using the Southern African Large Telescope with 1 week cadence over two sets of 4 month-long observing period, while we obtained 80 epochs of B band imaging data using a few 1-m class telescopes. Due to the seven month gap between the two observing periods, we separately measured the time lags of broad emission lines including H , by comparing the emission line light curve with the B band continuum light curve using the cross-correlation function techniques. We determined the H lag, _ cent = 8.46^ +2.08 _ 2.14 days in the observed-frame based on Year 2 data, while the time lag from Year 1 data was not reliably determined.

As a pilot study with the Southern African Large Telescope (SALT), which provides the unique capability of quasi-daily access, efficient service observations, and large photon-collecting power, we performed a reverberation mapping campaign of a nearby Seyfert 1 galaxy PG 0934+013 at R.A. = 09:37:1.0, Dec. = + 01:05:43, and redshift z = 0.0503 Veroncetty2001 . The target is also known as Mrk 707, which was classified as a narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy since the FWHM of the \ line was reported to be less than 2000 \ e.g., 1320 \ by BG92 . The host galaxy is a spiral galaxy with clear signs of spiral arms and a bar structure (see S 4.2).

sources.summary: The paper reports that PG 0934+013 is compact at uGMRT resolution but has a weak southern VLA extension, steep-spectrum core, offset polarized knots, and ambiguous jet, lobe, or wind origin.

quotes.quote: PG 0934+013 appears compact in the uGMRT image while its VLA image exhibits a weak extension (a 2 feature) to the south. The 2-frequency uGMRT-VLA spectral index image shows a mean spectral index of 0.5 0.1 in the uGMRT core; this value is at the cusp of flat-steep division. The in-band VLA spectral index image of PG 0934+013 exhibits a steep spectrum core. PG 1011 040 shows a marginal elongation ( 1.8 feature) in the northwest direction in the VLA image, while it appears compact in the uGMRT image. Interestingly, the 2-frequency uGMRT-VLA spectral index image of PG 1011 040 exhibits a flat spectrum uGMRT core, whereas its in-band VLA spectral index image reveals a steep spectrum core. We note that the weak extensions in PG0934+013 and PG1011-040 must be treated with caution and need to be confirmed with deeper observations. Nevertheless, the spectral index properties of PG 0923+129, PG 0934+013, and PG 1011 040 suggest the presence of either a sub-arcsec scale jet or lobe emission or optically thin synchrotron emission from an AGN wind Hwang18 . Moreover, since these sources were found to lie on the radio-IR correlation in our uGMRT study Silpa20 , we cannot completely rule out the emission from stellar-related processes in them. PG 1119+120 appears compact in the uGMRT image. Its VLA image detects a triple radio structure comprising of a lobe-core-hotspot in the NE-SW direction. This is consistent with the spectral index gradient detected at 6 level (based on the spectral index slices) in the 2-frequency uGMRT-VLA spectral index image of its uGMRT core. The in-band VLA spectral index image of this source reveals a flat spectrum in the core. PG 1211+143 appears compact in both uGMRT and VLA images. We had concluded from our uGMRT study that the AGN rather than the stellar activity was the primary contributor of radio emission in this source Silpa20 . The 2-frequency uGMRT-VLA spectral index image reveals a marginally steep spectrum ( _ 2-freq = -0.59 0.07 ) uGMRT core, which could suggest either jet or lobe emission on sub-arcsec scales or optically thin synchrotron emission from an AGN-driven wind. Interestingly, the in-band VLA spectral index for the core is 0.5 0.1 which is at the cusp of flat-steep division. This could suggest that the steeper spectrum component associated with the radio outflow may have been missed out by the higher-resolution VLA observations and what is detected here is the synchrotron self-absorbed base of a low-power or `frustrated' jet. The uGMRT images of PG 1229+204 and PG 1244+026 reveal kpc-scale curved jets or lobe-like emission to the south. The VLA image of PG 1229+204 also detects extended emission in the same direction, resembling a curved jet. This extension is weak however, and should be treated with caution. The VLA image of PG 1244+026 reveals only a compact core. The 2-frequency uGMRT-VLA spectral index image of PG 1229+204 reveals a flat spectrum in the uGMRT core whereas its in-band VLA spectral index image reveals a steep spectrum VLA core. The 2-frequency uGMRT-VLA spectral index image detects a steep spectrum uGMRT core in PG 1244+026. PG 1426+015 appears compact in both the uGMRT and VLA images, and exhibits a flat spectrum uGMRT core in its 2-frequency uGMRT-VLA spectral index image. The in-band VLA spectral index images of PG 1244+026 and PG 1426+015 reveal flat spectrum in their cores, which could suggest synchrotron self-absorbed bases of small-scale jets (such as on sub-arcsec scales). Within errors, the possibility of flat spectrum ( _ in-band = -0.11 0.08 ) arising from the thermal free-free emission processes from AGN or HII regions cannot be ruled out in PG 1244+026. Radio Polarization Properties of Individual Sources Out of the 11 sources, we detect marginal linear polarization in four sources, viz., PG 0050+124, PG 0923+129, PG 0934+013, and PG 1229+204; this needs confirmation with deeper observations. The fractional polarization varies between 2 and 25 in them. The polarised emission in PG 0050+124 is found to be offset from the core; this could suggest jet-medium interaction. The in-band VLA spectral index image suggests this region to be optically thin. Therefore, the inferred B-fields are perpendicular to the vectors in this region. PG 0923+129 shows two distinctly polarized regions, one in the core and the other slightly offset from the core (annotated as C1 and C2 in Figure fig:2 , top right panel, respectively). The in-band VLA spectral index image reveals the C1 component to be optically thin and the C2 component to be optically thick. Since it is difficult to establish the origin of radio emission (among jet/AGN wind/starburst wind; see Section morphology ) in this source with the current data, we cannot as yet ascertain the relationship between the B-field structures and the direction of the radio outflow. Nevertheless, the polarization structures suggest the presence of a marginally resolved bent jet on the basis of (i) the drastically varying orientation of the EVPA vectors across the polarized knots, and (ii) the suggestion of jets in quasars being threaded by poloidal magnetic fields in the literature Miller12, MehdipourCostantini19 as well as from our previous polarization studies Silpa21a, Silpa21b, Silpa22 . Interestingly, the recent VLBA study by Chen23 has revealed three discrete components in PG 0923+129 at 1.4 and 4.9 GHz. The central component exhibits a flat spectrum, suggesting core emission while the extended components reveal a steep spectrum, most likely arising from a radio outflow. The polarized knots in PG 0934+013 and PG 1229+204 are slightly offset from their radio cores. From their in-band spectral index images, the polarized region is marginally steep in PG 0934+013 and very steep in PG 1229+204, suggesting these regions to be optically thin.

The inferred B-fields in PG 0934+013 are transverse to the tentative jet feature to the south. Recent studies of RQ sources with the uGMRT and VLA have reported toroidal B-fields in the kpc-scale radio cores Silpa21a, Silpa21b, Silpa22 . In PG 1229+204, the inferred B fields are parallel to the direction of the southern jet. It is worth noting that poloidal B-fields in the jet have been observed in III Zw 2 Silpa21a and Mrk 231 Silpa21b as well as in the RL PG quasars Baghel2023 . Origin of Radio Emission and Jet - Medium Interaction Based on the multi-frequency, multi-resolution radio data from the uGMRT, VLA and VLBA, we deduce that the radio emission in PG 0003+199 and PG 0050+124 is most likely associated with sub-arcsec scale jets. The radio emission in PG 0049+171, PG 1211+143, PG 1244+026 and PG 1426+015 is likely to be arising from the synchrotron self-absorbed base of a low-power or ‘frustrated' jet. Within errors, the possibility of stellar or AGN-related thermal free-free emission processes cannot be ruled out in PG1244+026. Signatures of jet emission are revealed by the uGMRT-VLA study in PG 0923+129, PG 1119+120 and PG 1229+204. The current study cannot discriminate between unresolved jet or lobe emission and emission arising from AGN or starburst-driven wind in PG 0934+013 and PG 1011 040.

sources.summary: PG 0934+013 hosts a disk galaxy with a prominent bar, spiral arms, and a difficult-to-measure faint B-band bulge component.

quotes.quote: PG 0934+013: The host galaxy contains an obvious disk with a prominent bar and spiral arms. The bulge is fit with n set free. The bulge component in the F438W filter is very faint and difficult to fit; its magnitude is estimated by iteratively adjusting the amplitude of the F814W model until the residuals appear acceptable. The B band magnitude of the bulge is likely more uncertain than in other sources, although the final B-I color ( 2.20 0.70 mag) does not seem unusual.

sources.summary: The paper finds PG 0934+013 has clumpy spiral-arm-like H regions, central bar star formation, a biconical AGN-ionized zone, and widespread LINER-like emission.

quotes.quote: PG 0934+013 : Two outer, clumpy, spiral arm-like regions can be clearly seen in the H map. The central zone, where a bar is present, also emits H , which, according to the BPT maps is powered by star formation activity. The zones ionized by the AGN are mainly distributed from the north-east to the south-west in the form of a biconical ionization cone. The [ S ii ] /H BPT diagram, however, suggests that LINER-like emission is ubiquitous across the host galaxy.

gmw_00001767.name: PG 1011 040
gmw_00001767.overview: PG 1011 040 is a radio-quiet quasar [arXiv:2006.01700] featuring a central bulge-like component, a stellar bar, and a clumpy, ring-like structure with star-forming clumps [arXiv:2101.00764, arXiv:2206.13189]. Its weak radio emission exhibits a steep-spectrum core [arXiv:2006.01700, arXiv:2301.07929] and may originate from a sub-arcsecond jet, lobe, or wind [arXiv:2301.07929].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J1014 0418 as a radio-quiet PG-quasar-sample target with slightly extended kpc-scale radio structure, weak luminosity, mean black-hole mass, and steep spectrum.

quotes.quote: J1014 0418 (PG 1011 040) This target is in the PG quasar sample Boroson1992 . It is a RQ source having a slightly extended structure at kpc-scale (Fig. p left panel) and a flux density of S_ int 0.38 mJy. The radio power is relatively weak with a luminosity of L_ int 38.23. The black hole mass is (M_ BH / M_ ) 6.86 which is at a mean level and in agreement with previous study Davis2011 . The spectral slope is steep with _ in-band 0.79.

sources.summary: The paper finds PG 1011 040 has marginal VLA elongation, contrasting flat and steep spectral-index cores, and radio emission whose jet, lobe, AGN-wind, or starburst-wind origin remains unresolved.

quotes.quote: PG 1011 040 shows a marginal elongation ( 1.8 feature) in the northwest direction in the VLA image, while it appears compact in the uGMRT image. Interestingly, the 2-frequency uGMRT-VLA spectral index image of PG 1011 040 exhibits a flat spectrum uGMRT core, whereas its in-band VLA spectral index image reveals a steep spectrum core. We note that the weak extensions in PG0934+013 and PG1011-040 must be treated with caution and need to be confirmed with deeper observations. Nevertheless, the spectral index properties of PG 0923+129, PG 0934+013, and PG 1011 040 suggest the presence of either a sub-arcsec scale jet or lobe emission or optically thin synchrotron emission from an AGN wind Hwang18 . Moreover, since these sources were found to lie on the radio-IR correlation in our uGMRT study Silpa20 , we cannot completely rule out the emission from stellar-related processes in them. PG 1119+120 appears compact in the uGMRT image. Its VLA image detects a triple radio structure comprising of a lobe-core-hotspot in the NE-SW direction. This is consistent with the spectral index gradient detected at 6 level (based on the spectral index slices) in the 2-frequency uGMRT-VLA spectral index image of its uGMRT core. We had concluded from our uGMRT study that the AGN rather than the stellar activity was the primary contributor of radio emission in this source Silpa20 . The 2-frequency uGMRT-VLA spectral index image reveals a marginally steep spectrum ( _ 2-freq = -0.59 0.07 ) uGMRT core, which could suggest either jet or lobe emission on sub-arcsec scales or optically thin synchrotron emission from an AGN-driven wind. Interestingly, the in-band VLA spectral index for the core is 0.5 0.1 which is at the cusp of flat-steep division. This could suggest that the steeper spectrum component associated with the radio outflow may have been missed out by the higher-resolution VLA observations and what is detected here is the synchrotron self-absorbed base of a low-power or `frustrated' jet. The uGMRT images of PG 1229+204 and PG 1244+026 reveal kpc-scale curved jets or lobe-like emission to the south. The VLA image of PG 1229+204 also detects extended emission in the same direction, resembling a curved jet. This extension is weak however, and should be treated with caution. The VLA image of PG 1244+026 reveals only a compact core. The 2-frequency uGMRT-VLA spectral index image of PG 1229+204 reveals a flat spectrum in the uGMRT core whereas its in-band VLA spectral index image reveals a steep spectrum VLA core. The 2-frequency uGMRT-VLA spectral index image detects a steep spectrum uGMRT core in PG 1244+026. PG 1426+015 appears compact in both the uGMRT and VLA images, and exhibits a flat spectrum uGMRT core in its 2-frequency uGMRT-VLA spectral index image. The in-band VLA spectral index images of PG 1244+026 and PG 1426+015 reveal flat spectrum in their cores, which could suggest synchrotron self-absorbed bases of small-scale jets (such as on sub-arcsec scales). Within errors, the possibility of flat spectrum ( _ in-band = -0.11 0.08 ) arising from the thermal free-free emission processes from AGN or HII regions cannot be ruled out in PG 1244+026. The in-band VLA spectral index image reveals the C1 component to be optically thin and the C2 component to be optically thick. The central component exhibits a flat spectrum, suggesting core emission while the extended components reveal a steep spectrum, most likely arising from a radio outflow. The polarized knots in PG 0934+013 and PG 1229+204 are slightly offset from their radio cores. From their in-band spectral index images, the polarized region is marginally steep in PG 0934+013 and very steep in PG 1229+204, suggesting these regions to be optically thin.
