sources.summary: PG 1011-040 is presented as relatively X-ray weak, with UV C 4 absorption but no strong BAL indication, little X-ray absorption, and no large X-ray luminosity variation across observations.

quotes.quote: Another source, PG 1011-040 , is relatively X-ray weak by a factor of 10 blw00, gblemwi01 . There is some evidence for C 4 absorption in the UV spectrum, but no strong indication of a BAL blw00, lb02 . It appears to be relatively unabsorbed in X-rays , with an upper limit on the absorbing column density of N_H 5 10^ 21 cm ^ 2 . In contrast to PHL 1811, multiple observations of PG 1011-040 have not found much X-ray luminosity variation gblemwi01 .

sources.summary: The paper describes PG 1011 040 as having complex CO morphology, connected bulge-like and clumpy ring-like structures, and possible molecular inflow linked to a stellar bar.

quotes.quote: PG 1011 040: The complex CO(2--1) morphology can be roughly separated into a central bulge-like component surrounded by a ring-like, clumpy structure. The LOS velocity map shows complex dynamics with a smooth variation of the velocity field across the field-of-view. This suggests that the two main structures are spatially connected. Comparison with the restframe I band HST/WFC3 image reveals a possible spatial correlation between the molecular gas distribution and the stellar bar structure, suggesting molecular gas inflow toward the central zone due to gravitational torques.

sources.summary: The paper describes PG 1011 040 as a quasar host with nuclear and ring-like H emission, large star-forming clumps, connected velocity structure, and a stellar bar.

quotes.quote: PG 1011 040: The H emission traces two main zones, a nuclear region surrounded by ring-like clumpy structure. Five large star-forming clumps are clearly seen inside the ring. The LOS velocity field is complex, but the velocity gradient varies smoothly across the field-of-view, suggesting that both structures are spatially connected. This is also supported by the rest-frame I band HST/WF3 image and MUSE white-light image, which reveals a stellar bar crossing from the ring-like structure from one extreme to the other through the nuclear region.

gmw_00001768.name: PG 1126 041
gmw_00001768.overview: PG 1126 041 is a disk-like active galaxy [arXiv:2206.13189] classified as a radio-quiet active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2009.11294] or mini-broad absorption line quasar [arXiv:1112.3750]. The system features a central bar-like structure [arXiv:2101.00764], nuclear star formation [arXiv:astro-ph/0407077], and highly variable, ionized X-ray and ultraviolet outflows [arXiv:2009.11294, arXiv:2306.05469].

sources.summary: The paper describes PG 1126-041/Mrk 1298 as a radio-quiet AGN whose UV and X-ray outflows are paired with MUSE evidence for regular disc rotation and modest momentum-driven ionised outflows.

quotes.quote: PG 1126-041 This object, also known as Mrk 1298, is a radio-quiet AGN, with a luminosity in between those of typical Seyferts and QSOs ( (L_ BOL / ) 11.62 ). As opposed to the previous system, in PG 1126-041 the outflow component was firstly detected in UV absorption using data from the International Ultraviolet Explorer (IUE) by Wang+99 , who reported outflow speeds in the range 1000-5000 , while only weak features were visible in the ROSAT X-ray spectrum. Later on, this QSO was the target of a multi-epoch observational campaign aimed at constraining the properties of its central engine with XMM-Newton Giustini+11 . These observations revealed a complex X-ray spectral variability which was previously noticed by Komossa+00 on time scales of both months and hours, and the presence of a highly ionised X-ray absorber at sub-pc scales with outflow velocity of about 0.055c , variable on time scales of a few kiloseconds.

In LV component in PG 1126-041 is likely associated to the regularly rotating disc of the galaxy, which does not seem to be perturbed by the nuclear activity. Outflows are characterised by small mass rates ( 0.6-4.3 ) and poor kinetic efficiencies ( 1-4 10^ 4 ). The momentum rates associated to the large-scale \ and \ outflows are compatible with those inferred from the sub-pc scale measurements in the X-rays, in agreement with a momentum-driven wind propagation.

sources.summary: The paper studies PG 1126-041 as a nearby active galaxy with coordinated UV and X-ray accretion-disk winds, variable absorbers, and properties close to Narrow Line Seyfert 1 galaxies.

quotes.quote: We aim to characterize the X-ray properties of the inner accretion disk wind of the nearby active galactic nucleus (AGN) PG 1126-041, and to study its connection with the ultraviolet (UV)-absorbing wind. We perform spectroscopic analysis of eight XMM-Newton observations of PG 1126-041 taken between 2004 and 2015, using both phenomenological models and the most advanced accretion disk wind models available. For half of the dataset, we can compare the X-ray analysis results with the results of quasi-simultaneous, high-resolution spectroscopic UV observations taken with the Cosmic Origins Spectrograph (COS) on board the Hubble Space Telescope . The X-ray spectra of PG 1126-041 are complex and absorbed by ionized material which is highly variable on multiple time scales, sometimes as short as 11 days.

PG 1126-041 is a low-redshift z=0.062 , 2009MNRAS.399..683J active galaxy with an optical magnitude of M_B=-22.8 1983ApJ...269..352S , very close to the threshold ( M_B=-23 ) historically used to divide Seyfert galaxies from the more luminous quasars, or quasi-stellar objects (QSOs). PG 1126-041 is ``almost'' a Narrow Line Seyfert 1 galaxy (NLS1), having very strong and very weak emission and a full-width-at-half-maximum (FWHM) of the H emission line of 2150 km s ^ 1 : only slightly larger than the classical FWHM < 2000 km s ^ 1 used to define NLS1s 1985ApJ...297..166O . The black hole mass is estimated to be M_ BH =1.2 10^8 M_ by 2007ApJ...657..102D using velocity dispersion measurements in the stellar absorption lines.

sources.summary: The paper finds disk-like and clumpy CO morphology in PG 1126 041, with bar-like central emission, stellar-gas spatial correlation, a flat rotation curve, and rising central dispersion.

quotes.quote: PG 1126 041: A clear disk-like morphology and rotational pattern can be seen. The central CO(2--1) emission seems to be distributed in a bar-like structure. The system also shows clumpy morphology.

The intensity map is spatially correlated with the HST/NICMOS image, suggesting that the stellar and molecular gas components follow similar distribution. The CO emission traces the flat part of the rotation curve, from which we measure a projected peak-to-peak rotational velocity of v_ rot i 390 km s ^ 1 . The velocity dispersion increases smoothly toward the galactic center, reaching 160 km s ^ 1 .

sources.summary: The paper resolves nuclear Pa emission in PG1126 041, interprets narrow-line excess as circumnuclear star formation, and estimates that this star formation can explain most of the far-infrared luminosity.

quotes.quote: In the quasar with the best adaptive optics correction, PG1126 041, we spatially resolve the Pa emission within the nuclear 100 pc. The comparison with higher excitation lines suggests that the narrow Pa emission is due to nuclear star formation. The inferred intensity of the nuclear star formation (13 M_ yr^ 1 ) may account for most of the far-IR luminosity observed in this quasar.

In PG1126 the ratio between narrow Pa / [ SiVI ] is 3.4 on the nucleus and 6.4 in the northern region at 60 pc from the nucleus. This comparison indicates that there is a strong excess of narrow Pa emission with respect to that expected from a NLR. Such a Pa excess is probably due to active star formation in the nuclear and circumnuclear region, within the central 100 pc.

sources.summary: The paper presents multi-epoch XMM-Newton observations of PG 1126-041, finding complex month-to-hour X-ray spectral variability from massive ionized absorbers and a faster highly ionized outflow that varies within hours.

quotes.quote: We present the results of a multi-epoch observational campaign on the mini-broad absorption line quasar (mini-BAL QSO) PG 1126-041 performed with XMM-Newton from 2004 to 2009. Time-resolved X-ray spectroscopy and simultaneous UV and X-ray photometry were performed on the most complete set of observations and on the deepest X-ray exposure of a mini-BAL QSO to date. Complex X-ray spectral variability, found on time scales of both months and hours, is best reproduced by means of variable and massive ionized absorbers along the line of sight. In the highest signal-to-noise observation, highly-ionized X-ray absorbing material outflowing much faster ( _ out 16500 km s ^ 1 ) than the UV absorbing one ( _ out 5000 km s ^ 1 ) is detected. This highly-ionized absorber is found to be variable on very short time scales of a few hours.

sources.summary: The paper describes PG 1126 041 as a disk-like quasar host with rotational H kinematics, clumpy outskirts, central bar/outflow-like emission, and AGN-ionized velocity-dispersion enhancements.

quotes.quote: PG 1126 041: This system clearly shows a disk-like morphology and rotational pattern traced by the H emission. A clumpy morphology is seen toward the galaxy outskirts, while in the central zone the H emission seems to be distributed in a bar-like or outflow structure. The velocity dispersion map is complex, with an enhancement of _ V, H toward the north-west and south-east, zones that are ionized by the AGN, although no clear substructures are visible in the H and [ O iii ] intensity maps. Ongoing star formation activity is necessary to explain the BPT maps.

sources.summary: The atlas treats PG 1126-041 as an individually observed active or starburst galaxy, summarizing its prior classification and near-infrared continuum, emission-line, and absorption properties.

quotes.quote: PG 1126-041 . The quasar PG 1126-041 is a strong X-ray source that shows clear signs of warm absorption wbb96 . Its optical spectrum rb84 is characterized by conspicuous Fe ii emission line complexes and broad Balmer lines. NIR information on this object is scarce, but recently cre04 reported adaptive optics assisted K band spectroscopy at a spatial resolution of 0.08'', allowing them to spatially resolve the Pa emission within the nuclear 100 pc. The comparison with higher excitation lines suggests that the narrow Pa emission is due to nuclear star formation.

sources.summary: The paper identifies PG 1126-041 as a mini-BAL quasar with iron-band highly ionised outflowing absorbers and hour-scale absorber variability.

quotes.quote: We note however how in two of the best S/N spectra it was possible to detect highly ionised outflowing X-ray absorbers in the iron K band, namely PG 1115+080 (Chartas, Brandt Gallagher 2003, Chartas et al. 2007) and PG 1126-041 (Giustini et al. 2011). These absorbers are found to be variable over time scales of years (PG 1115+080) and over very short time scales of hours (PG 1126-041).
Indeed, highly ionised iron is surely present in the inner regions of the mini-BAL QSOs PG 1115+080 and PG 1126-041.

gmw_00001769.name: PG 1426+015
gmw_00001769.overview: PG 1426+015 is a Seyfert 1 galaxy [arXiv:1908.07272] and luminous infrared galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0512055] undergoing a merger with a secondary starburst-dominated nucleus [arXiv:astro-ph/0512055, arXiv:2206.13189]. The system features a compact radio core [arXiv:2301.07929] and exhibits unusually strong X-ray variability [arXiv:1307.5253].

sources.summary: PG1426+015 is described as a LIRG/QSO merger system with two same-redshift nuclei separated by 4 kpc; the bright component is AGN-powered while the secondary appears starburst dominated.

quotes.quote: In one of the 12 QSOs targeted by our large program, PG1426+015, two nuclei separated by 4kpc appear in the acquisition image (also seen in HST NICMOS data; Schade et al., 2000). Our NIR spectroscopy indicates that the nuclei are at the same redshift. The optical spectrum of the bright component has very broad emission lines kaspi00 , verifying that the emission originating from this component can be attributed to an AGN. Calculation of the IR luminosity of the system (from IRAS fluxes) indicates that it is a LIRG.

The spectra of the QSO and the secondary nucleus are presented in Fig. fig:pg1426 . The second nucleus does not show any indications of strong AGN continuum and resembles those of the binary ULIRGs; therefore we believe that it is starburst dominated. This system constitutes a good example of how mergers can simultaneously trigger strong accretion and starburst events.

sources.summary: The paper adds 30 Swift observations of PG 1426+015 and finds unusually strong X-ray variability, including a short high-frequency break and rapid count-rate changes for its massive black hole.

quotes.quote: We obtained 30 Swift Gehrels2004a observations of PG 1426+015 every couple of days between 2013 April 2 and 2013 June 3.

The Swift XRT lightcurve for PG 1426+015 exhibits strong variability, and the amplitude of variability is unusual for a source with a M_ BH 10^9 M_ black hole. For this source we obtained a value of ^2_X = -5.49 0.37 \ fraction^2 / sec and _H 1 day. This is considerably more variable than expected for such a massive black hole. Moreover, the timescale corresponding to the high-frequency break is much shorter than the value of _H 30 - 100 days expected for M_ BH 10^9 M_ e.g., McHardy2006a,Kelly2011a,Gonzalez-Martin2012a . For example, between the 20--26 day markers in Figure f-pg1426_lightcurve the count rate triples over a couple of days, and then drops to half this value a couple of days later.

sources.summary: The paper describes PG 1426+015 as an interacting two-component QSO host whose NE nucleus is AGN-dominated; spectroscopy confirms the SW nucleus shares the QSO redshift, with a 4.4 kpc projected separation.

quotes.quote: PG 1426+015 is an interacting galaxy with two components. The data for both the bright, north-east (NE) nucleus and the secondary, south-west (SW) nucleus are presented here (see Figs. fig:spectra , fig:pg1426 ). The H- band spectrum of the NE nucleus is mainly dominated by the AGN continuum and it is 30 times brighter than that of the fainter nucleus.

Hubble Space Telescope (HST) imaging data presented by indicate the presence of a second nucleus south-west of the bright nucleus of PG 1426+015 (also see Fig. fig:pg1426 ). Our absorption-line spectroscopy of the host of PG 1426+015 confirms that the redshift of the second nucleus is identical to that of the QSO. The projected nuclear separation of the interacting galaxies is 4.4 kpc.

sources.summary: The paper describes PG 1426 + 015 as a Seyfert 1 with blackbody-like soft excess and weak, poorly constrained iron-band emission because the exposure is short.

quotes.quote: PG 1426 + 015 PG 1426 + 015 is a Seyfert 1 galaxy veron06 and shows a blackbody-like soft excess emission e.g. kT 0.1 keV, page04 . Fig. pic_fe presents a ratio plot of PG 1426 + 015 EPIC-pn spectrum fitted with an absorbed power-law model. The spectrum shows evidence for weak emission feature in the iron band and a strong soft excess emission. By fitting the emission feature of a zgauss model with a fixed line energy at 6.4 keV, we obtain an upper limit of the line width <0.40 keV. Although the spectrum shows tentative evidence for a broad emission feature, the short exposure of the observation does not allow us to better constrain the line shape.

sources.summary: The paper identifies PG 1426+015 as an ongoing merger quasar host with two tidal features, a rotating central disk-like component, star-forming tidal gas, and possible outflow/shock signatures.

quotes.quote: PG 1426+015: The host galaxy is an ongoing merger Kim2017 . Although the H emission map looks patchy, we can see clearly three different structures: a large tidal feature toward the east, a second tidal feature toward the west, and a central, rotating disk-like component according to the H LOS velocity map. The BPT maps suggest that star formation activity is probably taking place within the tidal features and in some small clumps located in the central disk-like component. The higher _ V, H observed toward the eastern zone of the inner disk-like component may suggest the presence of an outflow component and/or shocks affecting the ionized gas, judging by the [ S ii ] /H diagram.

sources.summary: The paper describes PG 1426+015 as compact in uGMRT and VLA images, with flat-spectrum core evidence consistent with the self-absorbed base of a low-power or frustrated jet.

quotes.quote: PG 1426+015 appears compact in both the uGMRT and VLA images, and exhibits a flat spectrum uGMRT core in its 2-frequency uGMRT-VLA spectral index image. The in-band VLA spectral index images of PG 1244+026 and PG 1426+015 reveal flat spectrum in their cores, which could suggest synchrotron self-absorbed bases of small-scale jets (such as on sub-arcsec scales). Within errors, the possibility of flat spectrum ( _ in-band = -0.11 0.08 ) arising from the thermal free-free emission processes from AGN or HII regions cannot be ruled out in PG 1244+026. Radio Polarization Properties of Individual Sources Out of the 11 sources, we detect marginal linear polarization in four sources, viz., PG 0050+124, PG 0923+129, PG 0934+013, and PG 1229+204; this needs confirmation with deeper observations. The fractional polarization varies between 2 and 25 in them. The polarised emission in PG 0050+124 is found to be offset from the core; this could suggest jet-medium interaction. The in-band VLA spectral index image suggests this region to be optically thin. Therefore, the inferred B-fields are perpendicular to the vectors in this region. PG 0923+129 shows two distinctly polarized regions, one in the core and the other slightly offset from the core (annotated as C1 and C2 in Figure fig:2 , top right panel, respectively). The in-band VLA spectral index image reveals the C1 component to be optically thin and the C2 component to be optically thick. Since it is difficult to establish the origin of radio emission (among jet/AGN wind/starburst wind; see Section morphology ) in this source with the current data, we cannot as yet ascertain the relationship between the B-field structures and the direction of the radio outflow. Nevertheless, the polarization structures suggest the presence of a marginally resolved bent jet on the basis of (i) the drastically varying orientation of the EVPA vectors across the polarized knots, and (ii) the suggestion of jets in quasars being threaded by poloidal magnetic fields in the literature Miller12, MehdipourCostantini19 as well as from our previous polarization studies Silpa21a, Silpa21b, Silpa22 . Interestingly, the recent VLBA study by Chen23 has revealed three discrete components in PG 0923+129 at 1.4 and 4.9 GHz. The central component exhibits a flat spectrum, suggesting core emission while the extended components reveal a steep spectrum, most likely arising from a radio outflow. The polarized knots in PG 0934+013 and PG 1229+204 are slightly offset from their radio cores. From their in-band spectral index images, the polarized region is marginally steep in PG 0934+013 and very steep in PG 1229+204, suggesting these regions to be optically thin. The inferred B-fields in PG 0934+013 are transverse to the tentative jet feature to the south. Recent studies of RQ sources with the uGMRT and VLA have reported toroidal B-fields in the kpc-scale radio cores Silpa21a, Silpa21b, Silpa22 . In PG 1229+204, the inferred B fields are parallel to the direction of the southern jet. It is worth noting that poloidal B-fields in the jet have been observed in III Zw 2 Silpa21a and Mrk 231 Silpa21b as well as in the RL PG quasars Baghel2023 . Origin of Radio Emission and Jet - Medium Interaction Based on the multi-frequency, multi-resolution radio data from the uGMRT, VLA and VLBA, we deduce that the radio emission in PG 0003+199 and PG 0050+124 is most likely associated with sub-arcsec scale jets. The radio emission in PG 0049+171, PG 1211+143, PG 1244+026 and PG 1426+015 is likely to be arising from the synchrotron self-absorbed base of a low-power or ‘frustrated' jet. Within errors, the possibility of stellar or AGN-related thermal free-free emission processes cannot be ruled out in PG1244+026. Signatures of jet emission are revealed by the uGMRT-VLA study in PG 0923+129, PG 1119+120 and PG 1229+204. The current study cannot discriminate between unresolved jet or lobe emission and emission arising from AGN or starburst-driven wind in PG 0934+013 and PG 1011 040. For the 11 PG RQ quasars studied with the VLA, we estimate the 5 GHz jet kinetic power using the MerloniHeinz07 relation. These values are provided in Table Table3 and they range from 10 ^ 42 10^ 43 erg s ^ 1 . The jets with these powers are typically susceptible to either stellar-mass loading e.g., Komissarov94, Bowman96 or the growth of Kelvin-Helmholtz (KH) instabilities triggered by recollimation shocks/jet-medium interactions e.g., PeruchoMarti07, Perucho14 . In the former scenario, the jets while propagating through the host galaxy, encounter numerous stars that inject matter into the jets via stellar winds. The injected stellar material mixes with the jet plasma, causing the jets to decelerate. Silpa22 had estimated the typical scales of the electron density fluctuations in an inhomogeneous Faraday screen which was most likely causing the depolarization of the lobe emission in a RQ quasar from the Quasar Feedback Survey QFeedS; Jarvis21 sample. Interestingly, the lower limit obtained on those scale sizes ( 10^ 5 parsec) matches the sizes of red giant stars, thereby suggesting the possibility of the stellar-mass loading effect in RQ quasars. KH instabilities also facilitate entrainment and mixing between the ambient gas and the jet plasma, causing the jets to decelerate and decollimate. KH instabilities could also result in jet bending e.g., Hardee87, Savolainen06 . Knotty polarization structures and the presence of poloidal B fields are typical signatures of KH instabilities e.g., in the `Teacup' quasar Mukherjee20, Silpa22 . Polarization is detected as discrete knots in our sources. The EVPA vectors within each knot have the same orientation while they differ between knots. Moreover, poloidal B-fields are inferred in the jet of PG 1229+204. These signatures might suggest the presence of KH instabilities in our sources. Nevertheless, there are no clear indications of knots in the total intensity images of these sources. Likewise, no modifications in the jet structure are observed at the sites of the polarized knots. Furthermore, the ability to distinguish discrete knots is crucial for the accurate identification of KH instabilities, which in fact, would depend on the resolution of the observations. Higher resolution observations in the future could test our interpretation of KH instabilities. Overall, therefore, the presence of localized or small-scale jet-medium interactions can be inferred in our VLA-observed sources from the jet kinetic power arguments as well as the polarization data. However, we do not find a correlation between the 685 MHz radio `core' sizes (Size ^ 685 _ core ) and [ O III ] 5007 luminosity (L _ [ O III ] ; _k = 0.037, p _k = 0.820) L _ [ O III ] is derived from the [ O III ] 5007 absolute magnitude, M _ [ O III ] , provided in BorosonGreen92 using the relation: M _ [ O III ] = 2.5 log _ 10 ( L_ [ O III ] L_ ), where L _ = 3.0128 10^ 28 W. for this sample. This correlation could have suggested an interaction of either the jets or the quasar-driven winds with the ISM e.g., Jarvis21 . A similar (though weak) association of larger radio sizes with higher [ O III ] luminosities has been observed in CSS/GPS sources e.g., ODea98, Labiano08, Kunert-Bajraszewska10 and RQ type 2 quasars Jarvis21 . The lack of correlation with the uGMRT core sizes could be the result of stellar contribution in the radio emission observed in the majority of these sources. We note that the VLA 5 GHz radio `core' sizes also do not show any correlation with L _ [ O III ] . Although these are small number statistics, if true, this may suggest that the sub-arcsec scale radio outflows in these sources may have been decollimated by jet-medium interactions. Looking for Signatures of AGN Feedback on Galactic Properties Our joint VLA-uGMRT analysis has established the presence of jets in 9 of the 11 sources. Recent simulations by Mukherjee18, TannerWeaver22, Meenakshi22 also show how initially collimated jets could produce isotropic effects and impact upon the gas and star formation properties in the host galaxies. Clearly delineated multiple jet episodes as well as AGN winds could also produce isotropic impacts Kharb2006, KingPounds15,Rao23 . Based on their morphology, spectral index, and polarization, a couple of sources from our VLA-uGMRT sample show signatures that could potentially indicate the presence of AGN or starburst winds. These signatures are seen in PG 0934+013 and PG 1011 040 among the VLA-observed sources, and in PG 1310 108, PG 1404+226, PG 2214+139 and PG 2304+042 among the uGMRT-observed sources see Silpa20 . The latter could be confirmed by future VLA observations. Therefore, we now briefly investigate if there is any signature of feedback on the galaxy-wide gas and star formation properties. For this we focus on the uGMRT data and examine the relationship between their low-frequency radio properties and star formation properties. The potential indicators of AGN feedback include quantities like CO luminosity, gas fraction which is the ratio of molecular gas mass (M _ H_2 ) and galaxy stellar mass (M _* ), depletion time which is the ratio of M _ H_2 and star-formation rate (SFR), and star-formation efficiency, which is the ratio of SFR and M _ H_2 . Gas fraction and depletion time are useful probes to determine the efficiency with which the stars are formed from a given molecular gas mass. By studying their correlations with the radio properties, one can learn about the impact of AGN on star-formation efficiency as well as their ability to remove or heat the molecular gas within the host galaxies. Here, we find the 685 MHz radio luminosity (L _ 685 ) correlates with ALMA CO(1-0) luminosity (L _ CO ; Kendall's tau correlation co-efficient ( _k ) = 0.427 and probability (p _k ) = 0.009), molecular gas fraction ( _k = 0.400, p _k = 0.014), molecular gas depletion time ( _k = 0.439 , p _k = 0.008), SFE ( _k = 0.439, p _k = 0.008) and SFR ( _k = 0.465, p _k = 0.006). The values of L _ CO , M _ H_2 , and M _* have been taken from Shangguan20b ; limits have been treated as detections for the correlations. These correlations, however, considerably weaken or disappear when the absolute accretion rate ( M ) is taken into account via partial correlation tests. We note that L _ 685 does not correlate with M _* ( _k = 0.063, p _k = 0.697) or BH mass (M _ BH ; _k = 0.127, p _k = 0.436); M _ BH values have been taken from Shangguan20b . The M values for individual sources are obtained from DavisLaor11 . These correlation plots have been presented in Figures fig:8 , fig:9 and fig:10 . We also note that, although these are small number statistics, the VLA 5 GHz luminosities do not show any correlation with L _ CO , molecular gas fraction, molecular gas depletion time, SFE and SFR. The L _ 685 SFR correlation is also a result of the mutual dependence of these quantities on L _ CO , as evident from the partial correlation test. We note that the SFR values used here are derived from the 8-1000 m host galaxy IR luminosity from Lyu17 following the Kennicutt98a SF law, which makes the SFR - L _ CO correlation very interesting. Our uGMRT study Silpa20 has already shown that the 685 MHz flux density has contributions from both AGN (jets/winds) and stellar-related processes (star-formation/supernovae/starburst-driven winds). Therefore, these correlations may have suggested that both AGN and starburst outflows impact the molecular gas. However, the dependence on M may rather suggest a close link between the inflowing gas and radio outflows. Another interesting correlation is the one between L _ CO and M , which suggests a connection between BH accretion and cold gas supply. This correlation has also been observed by other studies in the literature e.g., Diamond-Stanic12, Esquej14, Izumi16, Husemann17 . We note that Shangguan20b found a correlation between L _ CO and AGN 5100 continuum luminosity for a sample of 40 PG quasars which includes our sources. Global feedback signatures like the removal of molecular gas or quenching of star-formation are lacking in this sample as suggested by the previous studies in the literature e.g., Shangguan20b,Molina22a . However, it is important to note that a lack of molecular gas or SFR in luminous quasars does not rule out AGN feedback models, as implemented in the cosmological simulations of Ward22 . One reason why the removal of gas may not be apparent could be because the AGN may be impacting the ISM locally e.g., Mukherjee18, Jarvis20, Mandal21 . Another possibility could be that AGN feedback may be in its preliminary stage. The radio outflows may eventually impact the ISM, but they have currently been captured too early in the feedback process e.g., Jarvis20 . Another aspect to be considered is the evolution time-scales of the gas outflows versus the duration of AGN activity. Molecular gas outflows persist for a longer duration than typical AGN episodes. The ongoing gas outflow might not have been inflated by the current AGN episode. Subsequent episodes might also be illuminating the gas outflow e.g., King11, Zubovas18 . The lack of correlations observed between the radio and gas properties in the current work may be hinting at this. We are already finding indications of episodic AGN activity in a few of our sources based on their ultra-steep spectrum ( <-1 ) cores. In addition, our uGMRT study Silpa20 found that other than the correlation between L _ 685 and Eddington ratio, other 685 MHz radio properties did not correlate with BH properties like BH mass and Eddington ratio. This suggested the presence of either the stellar-related radio emission or radio emission from previous episodes of AGN activity (i.e. ``relic'' emission). Our new higher-resolution VLA images have most likely resolved out this relic emission. We also note that the host galaxies of a large fraction of the PG RQ quasars reveal signatures of ongoing galactic mergers Silpa20 . Galaxy major and minor mergers have also been proposed to be one of the plausible mechanisms for star-formation quenching in the literature in some cases, they are followed by AGN feedback; Hopkins06, Somerville08, Darg10, Smethurst15, Faisst17 . Galaxy mergers could also enhance star-forming activity in the host galaxies of quasars Sanders88, MihosHernquist96 similar to the `positive' AGN feedback mechanism Mahoro17, Mahoro19 . Overall, therefore, the merger scenario can also influence the interpretation of AGN feedback in the PG sources. We have presented results from our VLA polarization study at 5 GHz of 11 radio-quiet PG quasars. The primary findings from our work are summarized below. Our combined uGMRT - VLA study of the PG RQ sample shows evidence for low-power jets on sub-arcsec and arcsec scales in 9 sources, viz., PG 0003+199, PG 0049+171, PG 0050+124, PG 0923+129, PG 1119+120, PG 1211+143, PG 1229+204, PG 1244+026 and PG 1426+015.

sources.summary: The paper treats PG 1426+015 as an H-line-width outlier with an unusually low FWHM-to-line-dispersion ratio, noting it was also an outlier in the corresponding H figure.

quotes.quote: There are two H measurements not following the trend: the galaxies NGC 4593 and PG 1426+015. The galaxy NGC 4593 shows a high line width ratio FWHM/ _ line of 2.7. This might be explained with a high-inclination angle of the accretion disk (see Paper I) in this galaxy. PG 1426+015 shows a low FWHM/ _ line line width ratio with respect to the derived line width of 5450 km s^ 1 . The same galaxy was an outlier in the corresponding H figure (see Paper I).

sources.summary: The paper groups Mrk 1383 with Sy1 sources lacking absorption variability but having large covering factors and intermediate-to-high opening angles, preferring smooth gas with clumpy dust in a thick torus.

quotes.quote: Panel 3 of Figure fig:cartoon_Sy1 (e.g. Mrk 1383 and Mrk 590): Sy1 sources without absorption variability, large covering factors, intermediate-to-high half opening angles for both gas and dust. The gas is distributed smoothly while the dust is distributed into clumps. These two objects could be explained with a single geometrically-thick torus where dust is located in clumps while the gas is smoothly distributed

gmw_00001770.name: galaxy A
gmw_00001770.overview: Galaxy A is a group member located at a redshift of z = 0.45 [arXiv:2109.10875]. It is a passive system with a low star formation rate of less than 0.5 solar masses per year [arXiv:2109.10875].

sources.summary: The paper identifies galaxy A as a z=0.45 group member close to the DLA sightline, with low star formation, weak emission-line kinematic constraints, and likely interaction with galaxy B.

quotes.quote: More recent spectroscopic observations of objects A (impact parameter 8.8 kpc) and B (impact parameter 17.8 kpc) with the 8 m Gemini-South telescope revealed them to be galaxies whose redshifts fell within 100 km s ^ 1 of the neutral gas detected by ASKAP Sadler2020 . While this suggests that the H i absorption is likely to be associated with one of these galaxies, the exact nature and origin of the H i gas seen in absorption remained unclear.

For galaxies A, X and Y, the empirical relation in Kennicutt1998 without an extinction correction is used: SFR \ = (1.4 0.4) 10^ 41 \ L( [ O ii ] ) \ M _ \ yr ^ 1 . Due to the availability of prominent H and H lines in the spectrum of galaxy B, the [ O ii ] luminosity can be corrected for reddening. An intrinsic H /H = 0.47 Balmer decrement is assumed Osterbrock1989 to determine the emission-line colour excess and using the Kewley2004 relation, a SFR of 4.3 M _ \ yr ^ 1 is calculated for galaxy B. The other galaxies associated with the absorber have H emission nebulae with fluxes that are highly uncertain due to poor S/N or are below the detection limit. While galaxies A, X and Y are passive with SFRs 0.5 M _ \ yr ^ 1 , galaxy B has a star formation rate larger by roughly an order of magnitude.
Galaxy A has too low S/N emission lines in individual spaxels to be modelled using GALPAK ^ 3D , and its proximity to the quasar prevents accurate measurements of its photometric position angle.

gmw_00001771.name: galaxy B
gmw_00001771.overview: Galaxy B features a rotating ionized disc [arXiv:2109.10875] and exhibits an elevated star formation rate of 4.3 solar masses per year [arXiv:2109.10875]. It is located near and likely interacting with galaxy A, which is separated by only 17 kiloparsecs [arXiv:2109.10875].

sources.summary: The paper treats galaxy B as the main absorber-associated group member, finding a rotating ionized disc, elevated star formation, possible co-rotation or outflow, and interaction with galaxy A.

quotes.quote: This metal-line component aligns with the rotating ionized disc of galaxy B (impact parameter 18 kpc from the QSO) and appears to be co-rotating with the galaxy disc, although outflowing gas cannot be directly excluded. In contrast, the 21 cm H i absorber is blueshifted relative to the galaxies nearest the absorber and has the opposite sign to the velocity field of galaxy B. Since galaxies A and B are separated by only 17 kpc on the sky and 70 km s ^ 1 in velocity, it is likely that the 21 cm detection traces extragalactic clouds of gas formed from their interaction.

Due to the availability of prominent H and H lines in the spectrum of galaxy B, the [ O ii ] luminosity can be corrected for reddening. An intrinsic H /H = 0.47 Balmer decrement is assumed Osterbrock1989 to determine the emission-line colour excess and using the Kewley2004 relation, a SFR of 4.3 M _ \ yr ^ 1 is calculated for galaxy B.
From fitting of the ionized gas kinematics, there is clear rotation in the ionized disc of galaxy B.

gmw_00001772.name: X
gmw_00001772.overview: The emission line galaxy X is one of two newly identified members of a galaxy group [arXiv:2109.10875]. This group is aligned in a structure that may be a filament extending from the north-east to the south-west [arXiv:2109.10875].

sources.summary: The paper identifies X as one of two newly found group galaxies, but excludes it as directly related to the DLA because of its large impact parameter and velocity offset.

quotes.quote: In addition to galaxies A and B, which were known to be associated with the DLA from spectroscopy Sadler2020 , we find another two galaxies (X and Y) with impact parameters of 136 and 190 kpc respectively from the QSO sightline. This galaxy group is aligned in what may be a filamentary structure extending from north-east to south-west.

We hence exclude galaxies X and Y from having a direct relation to the DLA given their large projected separation ( 136 and 189 kpc respectively) compared to galaxies A and B, which have impact parameters < 20 kpc.
Galaxies X and Y are unlikely candidates due to their > 100 kpc impact parameter to the DLA and large velocity difference of + 297 and + 747 km s ^ 1 respectively to the absorber redshift.

gmw_00001773.name: Y
gmw_00001773.overview: Y is a distant galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.45 within a galaxy group aligned in a potential filamentary structure [arXiv:2109.10875]. Its spectrum features oxygen and hydrogen emission lines, as well as several higher-order Balmer lines [arXiv:2109.10875].

sources.summary: The paper identifies Y as a distant z=0.45 group member with emission and Balmer features, but argues it is unlikely to be directly connected to the absorber.

quotes.quote: In addition to galaxies A and B, which were known to be associated with the DLA from spectroscopy Sadler2020 , we find another two galaxies (X and Y) with impact parameters of 136 and 190 kpc respectively from the QSO sightline. This galaxy group is aligned in what may be a filamentary structure extending from north-east to south-west.

Emission lines [ O ii ] and H can be seen in all four group members as seen in fig:emission_fluxes , with H also prominent in galaxies B and Y. fig:balmer_lines reveals that the spectra of galaxies A, B and Y also feature higher-order Balmer lines ( H , H , H 9 , H 10 , H 11 and H 12 ).
Galaxies X and Y are unlikely candidates due to their > 100 kpc impact parameter to the DLA and large velocity difference of + 297 and + 747 km s ^ 1 respectively to the absorber redshift.

gmw_00001774.name: CG J0247+449a
gmw_00001774.overview: CG J0247+449a is a regular Sa/Sab spiral galaxy with an exponential bulge and no asymmetric structures [arXiv:astro-ph/0210510]. It hosts a Seyfert-2 nucleus, exhibiting a weak continuum, emission lines, and very low internal extinction [arXiv:astro-ph/0210510].

sources.summary: The paper describes CG J0247+449a as a regular Sa/Sab galaxy with an exponential bulge, weak continuum, emission lines, low internal extinction, and line ratios typical of a Seyfert-2 nucleus.

quotes.quote: CG J0247+449a. The morphological analysis in all four photometric bands indicates a regular galaxy without asymmetric structures. It is well fitted by an exponential bulge (n 1) and the B/T ratio is 0.37. No residuals are visible after model subtraction (see Fig. BVRI_r ). For this object (also known as 2MASXi J) we suggest a morphological type Sa/Sab.

The optical spectrum shows weak continuum and emission lines (Fig. spec ). [ O III ] 5007 is the brightest emission line, while Balmer hydrogen lines are partly absorbed by the underlying stellar continuum. After the subtraction of this stellar contribution by means of a template galaxy spectrum, following the prescriptions by hfs93 , we observe an H /H ratio 2.89, very close to the theoretical value of 2.86 for Case B recombination at electronic temperature T _e = 10 ^4 K ost89 , indicating very low internal extinction. The logarithmic ratios [ O III ] 5007/H = 0.95 0.34 and [ N II ] 6583/H = -0.30 0.18, even with large uncertainties, are typical of a Seyfert-2 nucleus vo87 .

gmw_00001775.name: CG J0247+449b
gmw_00001775.overview: CG J0247+449b is classified as an early-type E/S0 galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0210510]. It features a de Vaucouleurs bulge, disky inner isophotes, ring-like residual structures, and an absorption-line spectrum [arXiv:astro-ph/0210510].

sources.summary: The paper classifies CG J0247+449b as an E/S0 object with a de Vaucouleurs bulge, ring-like residuals, disky inner isophotes, and an early-type absorption-line spectrum.

quotes.quote: CG J0247+449b. This galaxy (2MASXi J) shows a B/T ratio 0.57, almost constant in all bands (see Table gim2d ). The bulge is well reproduced by a de Vaucouleurs ^ 1/4 law, therefore we classify this object as E/S0. The residuals show in each band a ring-like structure at a radius of 4 arcsec (better visible in the R-band in Fig. BVRI_r ) and a central X-structure aligned with the major and minor photometric axes, which indicates a disky shape of the inner isophotes schw98 .

Numerical simulations nb01 have shown that faint ellipticals with disky isophotes might be the final product of unequal mass disk galaxy mergers (mass ratio 3:1 or 4:1). The integrated spectrum of the galaxy shows only absorption lines and a continuum typical of an early-type galaxy (Fig. spec ).

gmw_00001776.name: CG J0247+449c
gmw_00001776.overview: CG J0247+449c is a lenticular or early-type spiral galaxy classified as S0/Sa [arXiv:astro-ph/0210510]. Optical spectrum analysis reveals a nuclear source and emission-line ratios that identify the galaxy as a LINER [arXiv:astro-ph/0210510].

sources.summary: The paper identifies CG J0247+449c as an S0/Sa galaxy with a nuclear source and emission-line ratios that confirm its LINER classification after stellar-absorption correction.

quotes.quote: CG J0247+449c. This galaxy (2MASXi J) has a B/T ratio around or even less than 0.4. Its bulge is well fitted by a de Vaucouleurs ^ 1/4 law like for object ``b''. So we classify it as S0/Sa. The residuals in VRI bands show the presence of a nuclear source, confirmed by the analysis of the optical spectrum.

Indeed, the spectrum has the continuum of an early-type spiral (see Fig. spec ) with deep metal absorption lines but with H , [ N II ] 6583 and [ S II ] 6724 emissions. Since the [ N II ] 6583 line appears more intense than H and the [ O III ] 5007 is barely detectable at noise level, we suggest that this galaxy could host a LINER. In order to avoid an underestimate of the H flux due to the underlying stellar absorption, we applied a template correction, as in the case of object ``a''. After this correction the two emission lines H and [ N II ] 6583 have comparable intensity. The diagnostic logarithmic emission-line ratios [ N II ] 6583/H and [ S II ] 6724/H are 0.22 0.10 and -0.16 0.15, respectively, confirming the classification as a LINER.

gmw_00001777.name: CG J0247+449d
gmw_00001777.overview: CG J0247+449d is a nearly edge-on, asymmetric Sbc spiral galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0210510]. Its faint optical spectrum displays weak emission lines, which indicate a normal level of star formation for a late-type spiral [arXiv:astro-ph/0210510].

sources.summary: The paper describes CG J0247+449d as a nearly edge-on, asymmetric Sbc galaxy whose faint spectrum and weak emission lines indicate normal star formation in a late-type spiral.

quotes.quote: CG J0247+449d. Object ``d'' is a galaxy seen almost edge-on ( i 80 ). Although it is generally not recommended for highly inclined galaxies, we attempted a morphological analysis in this case, as well. Despite the lack of visible residuals after the subtraction of the bidimensional galaxy model in the four photometric bands (Fig. BVRI_r ), the output values of the fit (Table gim2d ) are somehow puzzling, suggesting that the bulge-disk decomposition mostly failed. Therefore we limit our comments to point out that the galaxy is significantly asymmetric and the bulge seems to follow an exponential law. We propose a Sbc classification for this object.

The optical spectrum is very faint, but weak extended [ S II ] 6724 and H emission lines are measurable and their logarithmic ratio is -0.26 0.21, somewhat higher than expected for H II like galaxies. However the non-detection of [ N II ] 6583 and [ O III ] 5007 indicates a low ionization degree of the gas. The H emission-line is detectable at noise level. We interpret this emission spectrum as an indication of a normal level of star formation for a late-type spiral galaxy.

gmw_00001778.name: IRAS 02443+4437
gmw_00001778.overview: IRAS 02443+4437 is a close interacting galaxy pair with strong morphological distortions [arXiv:astro-ph/0210510]. The system consists of an early-type companion and a starburst spiral galaxy that dominates the unresolved far-infrared emission [arXiv:astro-ph/0210510].

sources.summary: The paper treats IRAS 02443+4437 as a close interacting S0+spiral pair with unresolved FIR emission, a starburst spiral component, an early-type companion, and strong morphological distortions.

quotes.quote: CG J0247+449e/f. These two galaxies form a close pair known as IRAS 02443+4437. Despite the application of the maximum entropy algorithm to the far infrared (FIR) raw data extracted from the IRAS database to approach the diffraction limit of the telescope, the galaxy pair could not be resolved. A single infrared source, centered at the position of galaxy ``f'', is detectable only at 60 (Fig. me ) and 100 m with measured fluxes 0.618 0.005 and 1.313 0.085, respectively.

The spectroscopic analysis reveals that the FIR emission is dominated by galaxy ``e'', which shows bright emission lines typical of a starburst galaxy. Measured fluxes of the emission lines (Table flux ) have been corrected for internal extinction A _ V = 1.61 determined assuming a theoretical H /H = 2.86 and applying the ca89 extinction law. The logarithmic diagnostic ratios [ N II ] 6583/H = -0.41 0.02, [ S II ] 6724/H = 0.51 0.04 , [ O I ] 6300/H = 1.61 0.13 , and [ O III ] 5007/H = 0.16 0.06 confirm the thermal nature of the ionizing source. The reddening corrected H luminosity L(H ) = 5.1 10^ 41 erg s ^ 1 corresponds to a SFR 4 M _ yr ^ 1 kenn98 . This value is lower than the SFR of the galaxy pair estimated from the FIR luminosity, but it only accounts for the portion of the galaxy covered by the slit.

gmw_00001779.name: galaxy A
gmw_00001779.overview: Galaxy A is a giant elliptical galaxy located at a spectroscopic redshift of z=0.079 [arXiv:astro-ph/0612351]. It follows a de Vaucouleurs surface brightness profile and is physically associated with a small satellite disk galaxy in a galaxy threshing system [arXiv:astro-ph/0612351].

sources.summary: The paper describes galaxy A as the giant elliptical member of a COSMOS threshing system, with surrounding satellites, a de Vaucouleurs-like profile, z=0.079, and large stellar luminosity.

quotes.quote: Galaxy A appears to be a giant elliptical galaxy while galaxy B appears to be a small satellite disk galaxy. It is remarkable that very long tidal tails are emanating from galaxy B. These features are quite similar to those found in the first galaxy threshing system reported by Forbes et al. (2003). Although we have not yet obtained optical spectroscopy of galaxies A and B, their physical association appears to be real, and thus this system is the second case of galaxy threshing.

In Figure 4, we show the surface brightness profiles of galaxy A as a function of ^ 1/4 . It follows a de Vaucouleur's ^ 1/4 law, suggesting that it is an elliptical galaxy, consistent with the SDSS spectrum.
We found a spectroscopic redshift of z=0.079 for galaxy A (SDSS J100003.2+020146.4) in the SDSS spectroscopic data archive of third data release (Abazajian et al. 2005).

gmw_00001780.name: galaxy B
gmw_00001780.overview: Galaxy B is a small satellite disk galaxy that is undergoing a tidal threshing event [arXiv:astro-ph/0612351]. This interaction has produced very long stellar tails and caused the galaxy to lose approximately half of its former mass [arXiv:astro-ph/0612351].

sources.summary: The paper identifies galaxy B as a small disk galaxy being tidally threshed by galaxy A, with long stellar tails, matching photometric redshift, and about half its mass lost.

quotes.quote: Galaxy A appears to be a giant elliptical galaxy while galaxy B appears to be a small satellite disk galaxy. It is remarkable that very long tidal tails are emanating from galaxy B.
In Figure 5, we show the surface brightness profile of galaxy B as a function of radius. It shows an exponential radial profile suggesting that this galaxy is probably a disk galaxy.

This suggests that stars located in the tidal tails were dispersed from the main body of galaxy B, providing strong evidence that galaxy threshing has been occurring in this system. The total magnitude of the tidal tails is comparable to that of the main body of galaxy B. We estimated that the luminosity of galaxy B is L_V (1.03 9.48 10^ 5 ) 10^9 L_ V, , and that of the tail is L_V (0.94 2.16 10^ 3 ) 10^9 L_ V, . These luminosities are nearly the same. This suggests that galaxy B lost about half of its former mass during the (on-going) threshing event.

gmw_00001781.name: J1324+4048
gmw_00001781.overview: J1324+4048 is a compact symmetric object candidate associated with a quasar at a redshift of approximately 0.496 [arXiv:1111.3710, arXiv:2009.02750]. The source features two steep-spectrum components separated by 32.4 parsecs, which exhibit loop-like hotspot motion and superluminal advancement [arXiv:1111.3710, arXiv:2009.02750].

sources.summary: The paper regards J1324+4048 as a CSO candidate quasar with two steep-spectrum compact components, symmetric morphology, and hot-spot motion but no detected flat-spectrum core.

quotes.quote: CSO candidate source J1324+4048 is associated with a quasar at redshift of 0.496(1 mas = 5.898 pc) . Figure fig:j1324 a displays the 8.4-GHz image observed on 2009 August 5. The source is characterized by two compact components along PA= 83 with a separation of 32.4 pc, in agreement with previous VLBI observations at close frequencies Hen95,Bri08 . These two components show a steep spectrum, i.e. , ^ 15.4 _ 2.3 =1.2 (eastern) and ^ 15.4 _ 2.3 =0.7 (western) (Paper II).

The symmetric morphology, edge-brightening and steep spectra of two components identify J1324+4048 as a CSO, even though a flat-spectrum core is not identifiable from these observations. However an alternative interpretation of a 'core-jet' source cannot be fully ruled out. In this scenario, the observed two components are bright knots in a single-sided jet, while the core, which is not detected in the present observations, lies at one end of the jet. In the current paper, J1324+4048 is regarded as a CSO candidate and the bright components are hot spots.

sources.summary: The review describes J1324+4048 as a compact quasar-associated peaked-spectrum source showing low radial velocity, loop-like hotspot motion, two steep-spectrum components, renewed feeding, and superluminal motion.

quotes.quote: For a redshift of about 0.5 or above, the lowest radial velocity from proper motion measurements is 0.12 0.04c for J1324+4048 An2016b .

Similarly, An2012b find evidence of a wandering hotspot in the CSO quasar J1324+4048 which has a loop-like motion, initially moving towards the north and then abruptly moving towards the south-west, reminiscent of the dentist drill model of Scheuer1982 for multiple hotspots.

Evidence of feeding is also seen in J1324+4048 a PS source associated with a quasar and with two steep-spectrum, resolved components, A and B, separated by 32 pc with no flat-spectrum core component. The flux densities of both components appear to decrease from 1993, reaching a minimum at 2005, consistent with expansion losses, and then found to increase by 40 per cent in 2009. In 1998 a new component was observed at the beginning of hotspot B which appears to be advancing with a superluminal velocity 2.2c along the axis, possibly feeding energy to the lobe An2012b .
